arXiv:2604.15536v2 [math.GT] 04 May 2026

Building homology theories (ala Floer)

John B. Etnyre School of Mathematics
Georgia Institute of Technology
Atlanta, Georgia etnyre@math.gatech.edu
Abstract.

These notes are an expanded version of evening talks at the 2025 Georgia International Topology Conference, and an abbreviated version of talks at Georgia Tech, which were aimed at graduate students. The hope was to indicate a common framework that has been used since the late 1980s to construct homology theories in low-dimensional topology and symplectic and contact geometry. In addition to this, we also try to indicate how the specific nature of the situation being studied dictates the algebraic nature of the chain groups used to define the homology.

The first author was supported in part by NSF Grant DMS-2203312 and the Georgia Institute of Technology Elaine M. Hubbard Distinguished Faculty Award.

  2. 2 Morse Homology

  3. 3 Lagrangian Floer homology

    3. 4.3 Applications
  5. References

1. Introduction

In the last 40 or so years, there have been lots of “homology theories” in topology and geometry that are built through a similar process. This goes back to work of Floer in [21, 23] that built Symplectic Floer Homology and Lagrangian Floer Homology by looking at the “moduli space” of solutions to a partial differential equation. Since then, the classical Morse Homology has been reinterpreted in this manner, see [42], and countless other homology theories have been defined. For example, various flavors of Instanton Floer Homology [7, 22, 34] and Monopole Floer Homology [33] are defined in a similar way, and Contact Homology [14], Embedded Contact Homology [30, 32], Legendrian Contact Homology [6, 11, 14], Heegaard Floer Homology [39], and Knot Contact Homology [12] are defined using variants of the Floer approach. (To be clear, the work to define each of these homology theories is considerable. While the approach was laid out by Floer, there are considerable details to be understood and dealt with.)

In these notes, we will discuss a general procedure for constructing the homology theories above. We will illustrate the process in three examples. The simplest case, with which we start, is Morse Homology. We then discuss Lagrangian Floer Homology and Legendrian Contact Homology. The main purpose of this paper is to (1) demonstrate the general process for constructing such homology theories and (2) give a good indication of how one must choose the correct algebraic structure for the chain groups of the homology theory based on the geometry of the problem at hand. There are several great references for the details of Morse Homology [31, 42], and some for the other theories [1, 2, 3], but we hope by not going into a lot of detail on the proofs (though Section 2.5 gives a flavor for these), we can help people see the general approach to building homology theories and how “compactness issues” force the necessary algebraic structures for the homology theory.

Before diving into the examples, we outline a general process for constructing invariants. Any unfamiliar terms in the examples below will be explained later in the text.

Before getting into the more complicated examples, we give a couple of very simple examples of the above process.

Example 1.1.

We will be interested in the topology of a smooth manifold MM. We carry out the above procedure as follows.

  1. Step 1.

    Choose a “generic” section of the cotangent bundle T∗​MT^{*}M.

  2. Step 2.

    Define a number by the signed count of the zeros of the generic section.

  3. Step 3.

    Show that this integer is independent of the “generic” section.

We call the number defined above the Euler characteristic of MM.

Exercise 1.2.

Rigorously go through Steps 1. to 3. to show that you have a well-defined integer associated with MM. If you know another definition of the Euler characteristic of a smooth manifold, show that it agrees with the definition above.

Example 1.3.

Suppose we are interested in the topology of knots in ℝ3\mathbb{R}^{3}. We carry out the above procedure as follows.

  1. Step 1.

    Choose a “generic” projection of the knot KK to ℝ2\mathbb{R}^{2} and remember over and undercrossings in the projection. (That is, choose a knot diagram for KK.)

  2. Step 2.

    Say the diagram DD of KK is 33-colorable if the strands of the diagram (that is the arcs the projection is broken into, when small neighborhoods of the crossings are removed from the undercrossing stand) can be colored by three colors so that at each crossing either one or all three colors are used and at least two colors are used.

  3. Step 3.

    Show that being 33-colorable is independent of the diagram chosen for KK.

Exercise 1.4.

Rigorously go through Steps 1. to 3. to show that being 33-colorable is an invariant of a knot KK in ℝ3\mathbb{R}^{3}.
Hint: Use Reidemeister moves. We note that the Reidemeister moves simply described a “generic” path of projection of KK.

Remark 1.5.

We note that there are lots of invariants of knots that can be defined diagrammatically, and so we see the process above to construct invariants is quite robust for knots. (We also note that many of these diagrammatic invariants — including 33-colorability — have other, more intrinsic, definitions that do not come from the above process.)

While this is a “high-level” look at defining invariants, we indicate how Steps 1, 2, and 3 go for “Floer-type homologies”. Above, we indicated what the chain groups would be, so we now need to consider what the boundary maps in the chain complex will be. This will necessarily be fairly abstract, but we will get into the details of the above homology theories in the following sections.

To carry out Step 1 and 2 we let ℳba\mathcal{M}^{a}_{b} be the space of “objects” counted in the differential (for example, for Morse homology they will be gradient flow lines from aa to bb, for Lagrangian Floer homology they will be holomorphic disk “from” aa “to” bb, and for Legendrian contact homology they are a bit more complicated). The first theorem we need is a transversality theorem that takes the following form.

To see why this is called a transversality theorem, we note that ℳba\mathcal{M}_{b}^{a} can be defined as the preimage under some function FF defined on some function spaces of a submanifold of the target space. So if FF is transverse to this submanifold (in some appropriate sense), the preimage will be a manifold. Next, we would like to know that ℳba\mathcal{M}_{b}^{a} can be oriented.

We would also like to know if ℳba\mathcal{M}_{b}^{a} is compact and if not, how it fails to be compact.

We first note that this theorem should really be considered a “lack of compactness” theorem since frequently the spaces are not actually compact, but it is still common to call this a “compactness” theorem. In all relevant situations, we will see that when ℳba\mathcal{M}^{a}_{b} has dimension 0, then ℳba\mathcal{M}_{b}^{a} is compact. Thus ℳba\mathcal{M}_{b}^{a} will be a finite number of points with signs, and we can define the boundary map for the chain complex to be

∂a=∑|ℳba|​b,\partial a=\sum|\mathcal{M}^{a}_{b}|b,

where the sum is over all bb such that ℳba\mathcal{M}^{a}_{b} has dimension 0 and |ℳba||\mathcal{M}^{a}_{b}| is the signed count of points in ℳba\mathcal{M}^{a}_{b} (we note if you only use the modulo two count of points in ℳba\mathcal{M}^{a}_{b} then one can skip Theorem 2 above). We will see below that the precise form of the compactness theorem will dictate the exact algebraic structure of the chain complex. The above theorems and comments indicate that ∂a\partial a is well-defined.

To show that ∂\partial is really a boundary map, that is, that ∂∘∂=0\partial\circ\partial=0, we need one last type of theorem.

In this abstract setting, it is not so clear how this will imply that ∂∘∂=0\partial\circ\partial=0, but below, in specific examples, we will see that this is indeed the case.

So the above four theorems are exactly what is needed to prove that we have a chain complex! To carry out Step 3, that is to see that the homology of this complex does not depend on the extra data chosen in Step 1, we need to prove analogs of Theorems 1 through 4 when one considers “generic” 11-parameter families of extra data from Step 1.

While the above discussion is a bit abstract, in the next section, we will see precise versions of the above theorems and prove that they allow us to define chain complexes whose homology is an invariant of manifolds.

We have tried to make these notes largely self-contained and accessible to anyone with a background in differential topology at the level of the standard textbooks [29, 35].

Acknowledgements

We thank Bülent Tosun and an anonymous referee for valuable feedback on early drafts of the paper. The author was partially supported by the National Science Foundation grant DMS-2203312 and the Georgia Institute of Technology Elaine M. Hubbard Distinguished Faculty Award

2. Morse Homology

In this section, we will study a compact manifold without boundary. We do not really need these assumptions on the manifold, but it is easier on a first pass. Once the reader has understood this case, they are encouraged to consider the necessary modifications for manifolds with boundary and non-compact manifolds. But for now

all manifolds in this section are compact and without boundary.

In the first section, we will recall facts about Morse functions and gradient flows, while in the second section, we will discuss the space of gradient flow lines. In the next section, we will define Morse homology, and in the final section, we will briefly indicate what goes into the proofs of the main theorems.

We would also like to point out that the approach to Morse homology given below fits nicely with our discussion of other homology theories, but is not the original way in which Morse homology was defined. There were several previous approaches that were much more in the spirit of topology and dynamics. We refer the reader to the beautiful exposition of Bott [4] on the history of Morse homology.

2.1. Morse functions and gradient flows

We recall that a function

f:M→ℝf\colon\thinspace M\to\mathbb{R}

is called a Morse function if any critical point p∈Mp\in M of ff — that is the points where d​fp=0df_{p}=0 — is non-degenerate. Here by non-degenerate we mean that the Hessian of ff at pp,

H​fp:Tp​M×Tp​M→ℝ:(v,w)↦w⋅(v⋅f),Hf_{p}\colon\thinspace T_{p}M\times T_{p}M\to\mathbb{R}\colon\thinspace(v,w)\mapsto w\cdot(v\cdot f),

is a non-degenerate symmetric 22-form. Here we think of a vector field as a derivation, so v⋅fv\cdot f is another function that can be thought of as the directional derivative of ff at each point.

Remark 2.1.

We note that, as defined above, the Hessian is not well-defined. This is because for a derivation v∈Tp​Mv\in T_{p}M, we have that v⋅fv\cdot f is a real number, not a function on MM, so we cannot apply ww to this. What we mean by the notation above is given v∈Tp​Mv\in T_{p}M, we extend vv to a vector field v~\widetilde{v} on MM so that v~⋅f\widetilde{v}\cdot f is another function on MM and then w⋅(v~⋅f)w\cdot(\widetilde{v}\cdot f) makes sense. But the value, in general, depends on the extension v~\widetilde{v} of vv.

Now at a critical point pp of ff we will see that H​fpHf_{p} is well-defined. To this end, let w~\widetilde{w} be an extension of ww to a vector field on MM and recall that

v⋅f=d​fp​(v).v\cdot f=df_{p}(v).

So we see that

(v~⋅(w~⋅f))​(p)−(w~⋅(v~⋅f))​(p)=([v~,w~])p⋅f=d​fp​([v~,w~])=0(\widetilde{v}\cdot(\widetilde{w}\cdot f))(p)-(\widetilde{w}\cdot(\widetilde{v}\cdot f))(p)=([\widetilde{v},\widetilde{w}])_{p}\cdot f=df_{p}([\widetilde{v},\widetilde{w}])=0

since d​fp=0df_{p}=0 at a critical point. Here [v~,w~]p[\widetilde{v},\widetilde{w}]_{p} is the Lie bracket of the vector fields evaluated at pp. Thus, we see that

(v~⋅(w~⋅f))​(p)=(w~⋅(v~⋅f))​(p)(\widetilde{v}\cdot(\widetilde{w}\cdot f))(p)=(\widetilde{w}\cdot(\widetilde{v}\cdot f))(p)

and so

(v~⋅(w~⋅f))​(p)=d​(w~⋅f)p​(v~)=d​(w~⋅f)p​(v)(\widetilde{v}\cdot(\widetilde{w}\cdot f))(p)=d(\widetilde{w}\cdot f)_{p}(\widetilde{v})=d(\widetilde{w}\cdot f)_{p}({v})

since d​(w~⋅f)p​(v~)d(\widetilde{w}\cdot f)_{p}(\widetilde{v}) only depends on the value of v~\widetilde{v} at pp. We can similarly see (v~⋅(w~⋅f))​(p)(\widetilde{v}\cdot(\widetilde{w}\cdot f))(p) only depends on the value of w~\widetilde{w} at pp and thus H​fp​(v,w)Hf_{p}(v,w) is well-defined when pp is a critical point. We also notice that H​fpHf_{p} is symmetric at a critical point.

Exercise 2.2.

In local coordinates (x1,…,xn)(x_{1},\ldots,x_{n}) near pp, show that the symmetric, bilinear form H​fpHf_{p} can be given by the matrix

(∂2f∂xi​∂xj)i,j=1n\begin{pmatrix}\frac{\partial^{2}f}{\partial x_{i}\partial x_{j}}\end{pmatrix}_{i,j=1}^{n}

in the basis ∂∂x1,…,∂∂xn\frac{\partial}{\partial x_{1}},\ldots,\frac{\partial}{\partial x_{n}} for the tangent space given by the coordinate system.

Exercise 2.3.

Show that the matrix expression for H​fpHf_{p} is independent of local coordinates at a critical point. (That is, show that in another local coordinate system (y1,…,yn)(y_{1},\ldots,y_{n}) you will get a different matrix, but it will be related by a change of basis for the tangent space given by the change of coordinates.)

Here is another way to define the Hessian.

Exercise 2.4.

Suppose that gg is a Riemannian metric on MM and let ∇\nabla be the Levi-Civita connection on T∗​MT^{*}M associated to gg (we note that the Levi-Civita connection is usually defined on T​MTM, but as T​MTM and T∗​MT^{*}M can be identified using gg we will consider the connection on T∗​MT^{*}M). We note that one may actually use any connection on T∗​MT^{*}M, but this is a natural one. We recall that ∇\nabla allows one to differentiate sections of T∗​MT^{*}M. So, given a vector field ww, we obtain the 11-form ∇wd​f\nabla_{w}df. Show that at a critical point pp of ff, ∇wd​f\nabla_{w}df is independent of the connection and H​fp​(v,w)=(∇wd​f)​(v)Hf_{p}(v,w)=(\nabla_{w}df)(v).

Exercise 2.5.

Note that d​f:M→T∗​Mdf\colon\thinspace M\to T^{*}M is a section of the cotangent bundle. Show that ff is a Morse function if and only if d​fdf is transverse to the zero section ZZ of T∗​MT^{*}M.

The first thing one would like to know is that any manifold has a Morse function.

Theorem 2.6.

A generic smooth function f:M→ℝf\colon\thinspace M\to\mathbb{R} is a Morse function.

In this theorem, by generic, we mean that the space of Morse functions ℳ​(M)\mathcal{M}(M) on MM as a subset of the set C∞​(M)C^{\infty}(M) of all C∞C^{\infty} functions on MM is dense. (Or more precisely, ℳ​(M)\mathcal{M}(M) is residual, meaning it is the intersection of countably many open dense sets in C∞​(M)C^{\infty}(M).)

There are many proofs of the above theorem. Most involve embedding MM into some Euclidean space, but see [27, Proposition 6.13] for an alternate and beautiful proof using jet transversality.

Given a Morse function f:M→ℝf\colon\thinspace M\to\mathbb{R} on an nn-manifold MM and a critical point p∈Mp\in M, the Hessian H​fpHf_{p} can be represented by a symmetric n×nn\times n matrix. Thus, the matrix can be diagonalized. This implies that

Tp​M=V0⊕V+⊕V−T_{p}M=V_{0}\oplus V_{+}\oplus V_{-}

where V+,V_{+}, respectively V−V_{-}, is the subspace of Tp​MT_{p}M on which H​fpHf_{p} is positive, respectively negative, definite and V0V_{0} is the subspace where H​fpHf_{p} is degenerate. Since ff is Morse and pp is a non-degenerate critical point, we see that V0={0}V_{0}=\{0\}, and so

n=dimTp​M=dimV++dimV−n=\dim T_{p}M=\dim V_{+}+\dim V_{-}

We call the dimension of V−V_{-} the index of pp.

Theorem 2.7 (Morse Lemma).

Let pp be a non-degenerate critical point of

f:M→ℝ.f\colon\thinspace M\to\mathbb{R}.

If kk is the index of pp then there is a coordinate chart ϕ:U→V\phi\colon\thinspace U\to V around pp such that

f∘ϕ−1​(x1,…,xn)=f​(p)−x12−⋯−xk2+xk+12+⋯+xn2.f\circ\phi^{-1}(x_{1},\ldots,x_{n})=f(p)-x_{1}^{2}-\cdots-x^{2}_{k}+x^{2}_{k+1}+\cdots+x^{2}_{n}.
Remark 2.8.

Recall from differential topology that if the first derivative of a function f:M→ℝf\colon\thinspace M\to\mathbb{R} is non-degenerate at a point pp, that is d​fp≠0df_{p}\not=0, then there are local coordinates where ff takes the form

ℝn→ℝ:(x1,…,xn)↦x1.\mathbb{R}^{n}\to\mathbb{R}\colon\thinspace(x_{1},\ldots,x_{n})\mapsto x_{1}.

Said another way, if the first derivative of ff at pp is non-degenerate, then ff is locally equivalent to the “simplest” function with non-degenerate first derivative.

One may think of the Morse lemma as a second derivative version of this. So if d​fp=0df_{p}=0 but H​fpHf_{p} is non-degenerate (that is the total second derivative is non-degenerate), then ff is locally equivalent to the “simplest” function with vanishing first derivative and non-degenerate second derivative.

There are several proofs of the Morse Lemma; see, for example, [2, 27, 37].

Given a Riemannian metric gg on a manifold MM and a function f:M→ℝf\colon\thinspace M\to\mathbb{R}, the gradient of ff is the unique vector field ∇f\nabla f such that

g​(∇f,v)=d​f​(v)g(\nabla f,v)=df(v)

for all vector fields vv.

Exercise 2.9.

Prove that ∇f\nabla f exists and is unique.
Hint: Recall that gg is non-degenerate and so the map Tp​M→Tp∗​M:v↦ιv​gT_{p}M\to T^{*}_{p}M\colon\thinspace v\mapsto\iota_{v}g is an isomorphism for all pp, where ιv​g\iota_{v}g is the contraction of vv into gg. That is, ιv​g\iota_{v}g is the 11-form that takes a vector ww and gives the real number g​(v,w)g(v,w).

The gradient flow of ff is the flow of −∇f-\nabla f. Specifically, given a point x∈Mx\in M the gradient flow line of ff through xx is

γ:ℝ→M\gamma:\mathbb{R}\to M

such that

γ′​(t)\displaystyle\gamma^{\prime}(t) =−∇fγ​(t)\displaystyle=-\nabla f_{\gamma(t)}
γ​(0)\displaystyle\gamma(0) =x.\displaystyle=x.

Since we are considering compact manifolds without boundary, the flow line is defined for all time.

Recall that the gradient of a function points in the direction in which ff is increasing the fastest. Thus, the gradient flow is going in the direction of decreasing values of ff. So if our manifold is the graph of some function hh and we think of ff as a height function on a manifold, then one can think of the gradient flow as the flow of water poured on the manifold.

Example 2.10.

Consider the manifold ℝn\mathbb{R}^{n} and the function

f​(x1,…,xn)=−x12−⋯−xk2+xk+12+⋯​xn2.f(x_{1},\ldots,x_{n})=-x_{1}^{2}-\cdots-x_{k}^{2}+x_{k+1}^{2}+\cdots x_{n}^{2}.

We also choose the Euclidean metric

g=∑i=1nd​xi⊗d​xi.g=\sum_{i=1}^{n}dx_{i}\otimes dx_{i}.

One easily computes that

d​f=−∑i=1k2​xi​d​xi+∑i=k+1n2​xi​d​xidf=-\sum_{i=1}^{k}2x_{i}\,dx_{i}+\sum_{i=k+1}^{n}2x_{i}\,dx_{i}

and thus

∇f=2​(−∑i=1kxi​∂∂xi+∑i=k+1nxi​∂∂xi).\nabla f=2\left(-\sum_{i=1}^{k}x_{i}\frac{\partial}{\partial x_{i}}+\sum_{i=k+1}^{n}x_{i}\frac{\partial}{\partial x_{i}}\right).

The gradient flow lines are indicated in Figure 1.

Example 2.11.

Consider the unit sphere S2S^{2} in ℝ3\mathbb{R}^{3} with the metric induced from the Euclidean metric on ℝ3\mathbb{R}^{3}. If the function f:S2→ℝf\colon\thinspace S^{2}\to\mathbb{R} is given by projection to the last coordinate, then we see that there are exactly two critical points N=(0,0,1)N=(0,0,1) and S=(0,0,−1)S=(0,0,-1). It is clear that NN has index 22 and SS has index 0. Moreover, the gradient flow is shown in Figure 2.

Exercise 2.12.

Show that a flow line of the gradient in the previous example through the point x=(cos⁡θ,sin⁡θ,0)x=(\cos\theta,\sin\theta,0) is given by

γ​(t)=(cos⁡θcosh⁡t,sin⁡θcosh⁡t,tanh⁡t).\gamma(t)=\left(\frac{\cos\theta}{\cosh t},\frac{\sin\theta}{\cosh t},\tanh t\right).

Hint: Show that in stereographic coordinates given by

ϕ​(u,v)=11+u2+v2​(2​u,2​v,u2+v2−1)\phi(u,v)=\frac{1}{1+u^{2}+v^{2}}(2u,2v,u^{2}+v^{2}-1)

the metric is given by

4(1+u2+v2)2​(d​u⊗d​u+d​v⊗d​v)\frac{4}{(1+u^{2}+v^{2})^{2}}(du\otimes du+dv\otimes dv)

or in polar coordinates

4(1+r2)2​(d​r⊗d​r+r2​d​θ⊗d​θ).\frac{4}{(1+r^{2})^{2}}(dr\otimes dr+r^{2}\,d\theta\otimes d\theta).

Show that in these coordinates, ff is given by r↦r2−1r2+1r\mapsto\frac{r^{2}-1}{r^{2}+1} and

∇f=r​∂∂r.\nabla f=r\frac{\partial}{\partial r}.

Thus the flow line through (1,θ)(1,\theta) is t↦(e−t,θ)t\mapsto(e^{-t},\theta).

Here are a few simple facts about gradient flow lines that follow from general facts about flows.

Exercise 2.13.

Show that if γ:ℝ→M\gamma\colon\thinspace\mathbb{R}\to M is a gradient flow line, then γa\gamma_{a} define by γa​(t)=γ​(t+a)\gamma_{a}(t)=\gamma(t+a) is also a gradient flow line (through the point γa​(0)=γ​(a)\gamma_{a}(0)=\gamma(a)).

Exercise 2.14.

If γ\gamma and η\eta are gradient flow lines with the same image (this happens if and only if they have one point in common), then there is some aa such that γ​(t)=η​(t+a)\gamma(t)=\eta(t+a).

2.2. Spaces of gradient flow lines

We will now define the Morse homology of a manifold. We begin by discussing the “moduli space of gradient flow lines”. Given a Morse function f:M→ℝf\colon\thinspace M\to\mathbb{R} and two critical points pp and qq of ff we define the space

ℳqp={γ:ℝ\displaystyle\mathcal{M}^{p}_{q}=\{\gamma\colon\thinspace\mathbb{R} →M​ such that\displaystyle\to M\text{ such that}
1) γ is a flow line of −∇f,\displaystyle\text{1) $\gamma$ is a flow line of $-\nabla f$},
2) limt→−∞γ​(t)=p, and\displaystyle\text{2) $\lim_{t\to-\infty}\gamma(t)=p$},\text{ and}
3) limt→∞γ​(t)=q}/ℝ\displaystyle\text{3) $\lim_{t\to\infty}\gamma(t)=q$}\}/\mathbb{R}

where ℝ\mathbb{R} acts on the gradient flow lines as indicated at the end of the last section. We can think of

ℳqp= the space of unparameterized gradient flow lines from p to q.\mathcal{M}^{p}_{q}=\text{ the space of unparameterized gradient flow lines from $p$ to $q$}.
Example 2.15.

We will consider the function and metric in Example 2.11 above. It is clear that there are only two critical points, NN and SS. The only non-empty moduli space is

ℳSN≅S1.\mathcal{M}^{N}_{S}\cong S^{1}.

To see this, notice that any gradient flow line intersects the equator of the sphere exactly once, and for every point on the equator, there is exactly one (unparameterized) flow line through that point.

Example 2.16.

We now consider a Morse function on the torus. One standardly draws the torus as, for example, the result of taking the circle of radius 11 about (0,2)(0,2) in the x​zxz-plane and rotating it about the xx-axis, see Figure 5. If we restrict the projection map ℝ3→ℝ:(x,y,z)↦z\mathbb{R}^{3}\to\mathbb{R}\colon\thinspace(x,y,z)\mapsto z to this torus we will not get a “generic” Morse function as discussed below, so in this example we will slightly skew this torus and let ff the restriction of the projection map to this torus. See Figure 3.

We note there are 44 critical points. A maximum, and hence index 22 critical point, at dd, a minimum, and hence index 0 critical point, at aa, and two index 11 critical points at bb and cc. A few gradient flow lines for ff are shown in the figure. As it is difficult to draw more flow lines in this figure, we represent the torus as a square with opposite edges identified in Figure 4.

There, we can draw more of the flow lines. We note that ℳbd,ℳcd,ℳab,\mathcal{M}^{d}_{b},\mathcal{M}^{d}_{c},\mathcal{M}^{b}_{a}, and ℳac\mathcal{M}^{c}_{a} all consist of 22 points, the first two spaces correspond to the blue lines and the second two correspond to the horizontal and, respectively, vertical orange lines. The spaces ℳcb\mathcal{M}^{b}_{c} and ℳbc\mathcal{M}^{c}_{b} are empty, and ℳad\mathcal{M}^{d}_{a} is the union of 44 open intervals, which are indicated by the purple curves in the figure.

The next example shows that the above theorem is not true for any metric and Morse function.

Example 2.17.

In Figure 5 we see the symmetric torus in ℝ3\mathbb{R}^{3}. If ff is the restriction of the zz-coordinate to the torus then we see the critical points and some of the gradient flow lines in the figure.

Note that ℳbc\mathcal{M}^{c}_{b} consists of two points, but if we had chosen a generic Morse function and metric (here the metric is induced from ℝ3\mathbb{R}^{3}) in the sense of Morse Theorem 1, then ℳbc\mathcal{M}^{c}_{b} would have dimension −1-1 and hence be empty. Thus it is clear that Morse Theorem 1 is not true for all Morse functions and metrics.

We now move to the second main theorem in the case of Morse homology.

We will clarify what we mean by “consistently” below when discussing “gluing”, but now turn to the “compactness” theorem (or, as mentioned above, it can really be thought of as the “lack of compactness” theorem).

We say that {γi}\{\gamma_{i}\} converges to a broken flow line if there are critical points

p=r1,r2,…,rl=qp=r_{1},r_{2},\ldots,r_{l}=q

such that index⁡rj>index⁡rj+1\operatorname{index}r_{j}>\operatorname{index}r_{j+1} and there are elements γ~j∈ℳrj+1rj\widetilde{\gamma}^{j}\in\mathcal{M}^{r_{j}}_{r_{j+1}} such that for all ϵ>0\epsilon>0 there is some NN such that for all i>Ni>N we have

image⁡(γi)⊂ϵ-neighborhood of ​(⋃j=1l−1image⁡(γj~))\operatorname{image}(\gamma_{i})\subset\text{$\epsilon$-neighborhood of }\left(\bigcup_{j=1}^{l-1}\operatorname{image}(\widetilde{\gamma_{j}})\right)

and

(⋃j=1l−1image⁡(γj~))⊂ϵ-neighborhood of ​image⁡(γi).\left(\bigcup_{j=1}^{l-1}\operatorname{image}(\widetilde{\gamma_{j}})\right)\subset\text{$\epsilon$-neighborhood of }\operatorname{image}(\gamma_{i}).
Example 2.18.

In Figure 6 we see a sequence of gradient flow lines γi\gamma_{i} shown as orange curves converging to a broken flow line given as the union of γ~1\widetilde{\gamma}_{1}, and γ~2\widetilde{\gamma}_{2}

The light green region is a neighborhood of the union of the images of γ~1\widetilde{\gamma}_{1}, and γ~2\widetilde{\gamma}_{2}, and we see that for large enough ii, the images of the γi\gamma_{i} are contained in the neighborhood. A similar figure will show that for large ii the images of γ~1\widetilde{\gamma}_{1}, and γ~2\widetilde{\gamma}_{2} will be in a small neighborhood of the images of the γi\gamma_{i}.

Corollary 2.19.

If index⁡p=index⁡q+1\operatorname{index}p=\operatorname{index}q+1, then ℳqp\mathcal{M}^{p}_{q} is a compact 0-manifold.

Proof.

There are no critical points with index strictly between index⁡p\operatorname{index}p and index⁡q\operatorname{index}q, so any Cauchy sequence in ℳqp\mathcal{M}^{p}_{q} must converge to a point in ℳqp\mathcal{M}^{p}_{q}. ∎

Corollary 2.20.

If index⁡p=index⁡q+2\operatorname{index}p=\operatorname{index}q+2, then

ℳqp¯⊂(ℳqp∪(⋃index⁡r=index⁡p−1(ℳrp×ℳqr))),\overline{\mathcal{M}^{p}_{q}}\subset\left(\mathcal{M}_{q}^{p}\cup\left(\bigcup_{\operatorname{index}r=\operatorname{index}p-1}\left(\mathcal{M}^{p}_{r}\times\mathcal{M}^{r}_{q}\right)\right)\right),

where ℳqp¯\overline{\mathcal{M}^{p}_{q}} is the “completion” formed by adding all broken trajectories.

Exercise 2.21.

Prove this corollary.

We finally turn to the final main theorem for Morse homology.

Remark 2.22.

Here is where we need the orientations from Morse Theorem 2 to be “consistent”. This means that we can relate the orientations on the moduli spaces as indicated in this theorem.

Example 2.23.

We consider the compactification of the moduli spaces for the skew torus in Example 2.16 above. We note that ℳad\mathcal{M}^{d}_{a} contains an open interval corresponding to the pink curves in Figures 7.

The compactification of this interval consists of the broken flow lines γ1∪δ4\gamma_{1}\cup\delta_{4} and γ3∪δ2\gamma_{3}\cup\delta_{2}. More generally we see that

ℳbd\displaystyle\mathcal{M}^{d}_{b} ={γ1,γ2},ℳcd={γ3,γ4},\displaystyle=\{\gamma_{1},\gamma_{2}\},\quad\mathcal{M}^{d}_{c}=\{\gamma_{3},\gamma_{4}\},
ℳab\displaystyle\mathcal{M}^{b}_{a} ={δ3,δ4}, and ℳac={δ1,δ2},\displaystyle=\{\delta_{3},\delta_{4}\},\text{ and }\quad\mathcal{M}^{c}_{a}=\{\delta_{1},\delta_{2}\},

and

∂ℳad¯=(ℳbd×ℳab)∪(ℳcd×ℳac).\partial\overline{\mathcal{M}^{d}_{a}}=(\mathcal{M}^{d}_{b}\times\mathcal{M}^{b}_{a})\cup(\mathcal{M}^{d}_{c}\times\mathcal{M}^{c}_{a}).

So we saw that ℳad\mathcal{M}^{d}_{a} consists of 44 open intervals and thus its closure consists of 44 closed intervals with boundary being 88 broken flow lines.

This example indicates that the proof of the following result.

Corollary 2.24.

If k=index⁡p=index⁡q+2k=\operatorname{index}p=\operatorname{index}q+2, then ℳqp¯\overline{\mathcal{M}^{p}_{q}} is a compact 11-manifold with boundary

⋃r​ of index ​k−1(ℳrp×ℳqr).\bigcup_{r\text{ of index }k-1}\left(\mathcal{M}^{p}_{r}\times\mathcal{M}^{r}_{q}\right).
Exercise 2.25.

Prove this corollary.

2.3. Constructing Morse homology

Let f:M→ℝf\colon\thinspace M\to\mathbb{R} be a Morse function and gg a metric on MM so that all the theorems in the past section hold. We define the chain groups for Morse homology to be

Ci=free abelian group generated by critical points of index i.C_{i}=\text{free abelian group generated by critical points of $\operatorname{index}$ $i$}.

We then define

∂i:Ci→Ci−1\partial_{i}\colon\thinspace C_{i}\to C_{i-1}

on the generators p∈Cip\in C_{i} by

∂ip=∑q​ of index ​i−1|ℳqp|​q\partial_{i}p=\sum_{q\text{ of index }i-1}|\mathcal{M}^{p}_{q}|\,\,q

where |ℳqp||\mathcal{M}^{p}_{q}| is the signed count of points in ℳqp\mathcal{M}^{p}_{q}.

Notice that Morse Theorem 1 and 3 above show that ℳqp\mathcal{M}^{p}_{q} is a compact 0-manifold, and Morse Theorem 2 shows that ℳqp\mathcal{M}^{p}_{q} is oriented. So ∂iq\partial_{i}q is well-defined. We note that if we considered CiC_{i} to be a vector space over ℤ2\mathbb{Z}_{2} then we could just take the mod 22 count of points in ℳqp\mathcal{M}^{p}_{q}, and then we would not need Morse Theorem 2 (or the part of Morse Theorem 4 concerning orientations).

Theorem 2.26.

With the notation above we have

∂i−1∘∂i=0.\partial_{i-1}\circ\partial_{i}=0.
Proof.

We note that

∂(∂p)\displaystyle\partial(\partial p) =∂(∑r​ of index ​i−1|ℳrp|​r)\displaystyle=\partial\left(\sum_{r\text{ of index }i-1}|\mathcal{M}^{p}_{r}|r\right)
=∑q​ of index ​i−2|ℳrp|​(∑r​ of index ​i−1|ℳqr|​q)\displaystyle=\sum_{q\text{ of index }i-2}|\mathcal{M}^{p}_{r}|\left(\sum_{r\text{ of index }i-1}|\mathcal{M}^{r}_{q}|q\right)
=∑q​ of index ​i−2(∑r​ of index ​i−1|ℳrp|​|ℳqr|)​q.\displaystyle=\sum_{q\text{ of index }i-2}\left(\sum_{r\text{ of index }i-1}|\mathcal{M}^{p}_{r}||\mathcal{M}^{r}_{q}|\right)q.

So the coefficient on qq is ∑r​ of index ​i−1|ℳrp|​|ℳqr|\sum_{r\text{ of index }i-1}|\mathcal{M}^{p}_{r}||\mathcal{M}^{r}_{q}|, but note this is exactly

|⋃r∈R(ℳrp×ℳqr)|=|∂ℳrp¯|.\left|\bigcup_{r\in R}\left(\mathcal{M}^{p}_{r}\times\mathcal{M}^{r}_{q}\right)\right|=|\partial\overline{\mathcal{M}^{p}_{r}}|.

Since ℳrp¯\overline{\mathcal{M}^{p}_{r}} is a compact 11-manifold, we know the signed count of its boundary components is 0. Thus ∂∘∂=0\partial\circ\partial=0. ∎

We now give a “picture proof” of this result. This is the version most people would give!

Picture Proof.

We begin by noting on the left-hand side of Figure 8 we see how qq would show up in ∂2p\partial^{2}p. That is, it will show up as a broken flow line.

But Morse Theorem 4 says that this will be a boundary component of ℳqp¯\overline{\mathcal{M}^{p}_{q}} as indicated in the middle of the figure. This component of ℳqp¯\overline{\mathcal{M}^{p}_{q}} is an oriented 11-dimensional manifold. So this component of ℳqp¯\overline{\mathcal{M}^{p}_{q}} will look like the figure on the right of Figure 8. So you see if qq is in ∂2p\partial^{2}p then it will show up again with the opposite sign. Some people will just draw the diagram on the right of Figure 8 and call that the proof that ∂2=0\partial^{2}=0, and once you have thought through it, it is a great proof! ∎

We can now define the ithi^{\text{th}} Morse homology of MM to be

Hi​(M)=ker⁡(∂:Ci→Ci−1)image⁡(∂:Ci+1→Ci).H_{i}(M)=\frac{\ker\left(\partial\colon\thinspace C_{i}\to C_{i-1}\right)}{\operatorname{image}\left(\partial\colon\thinspace C_{i+1}\to C_{i}\right)}.
Exercise 2.27.

Show that Hi​(M)H_{i}(M) agrees with the ithi^{\text{th}} homology of MM as is usually defined in algebraic topology.
Hint: This might be more challenging than some previous exercises. Maybe read Section 2.5.5 first and think about cellular homology and how you might get a C​WCW structure on MM from a Morse function.

Of course, if you finish this exercise, we know that the Morse homology does not depend on the choice of Morse function or the Riemannian metric, but we now turn to a more direct proof of this fact.

2.4. Invariance of Morse homology

Suppose that (f0,g0)(f_{0},g_{0}) and (f1,g1)(f_{1},g_{1}) are two pairs of Morse functions and Riemannian metrics so that all the theorems in the previous section hold. Each (fi,gi)(f_{i},g_{i}) gives us a chain complex (Ci,∂i)(C^{i},\partial^{i}). We want to build a chain map

Φ:(C0,∂0)→(C1,∂1)\Phi\colon\thinspace(C^{0},\partial^{0})\to(C^{1},\partial^{1})

that will induce an isomorphism on homology. To this end, let

Γ={(ft,gt):\displaystyle\Gamma=\{(f_{t},g_{t})\colon\thinspace t∈ℝ​ such that\displaystyle t\in\mathbb{R}\text{ such that }
ft=f0​ and ​gt=g0​ for ​t≤0\displaystyle f_{t}=f_{0}\text{ and }g_{t}=g_{0}\text{ for }t\leq 0
ft=f1 and gt=g1 for t≥1}.\displaystyle f_{t}=f_{1}\text{ and }g_{t}=g_{1}\text{ for }t\geq 1\}.

We note that the space of functions and the space of metrics are contractible, so such a Γ\Gamma exists. Now let VtV_{t} be the gradient ∇gtft\nabla_{g_{t}}f_{t} of ftf_{t} with respect to the metric gtg_{t} and set VV to be the time-dependent vector field such that at time tt the vector field VV is −Vt-V_{t}:

V={−Vt}.V=\{-V_{t}\}.

A flow line of VV is a map γ:ℝ→M\gamma\colon\thinspace\mathbb{R}\to M such that γ′​(t)=(Vt)γ​(t)\gamma^{\prime}(t)=(V_{t})_{\gamma(t)}. Now given a critical point pp of f0f_{0} and a critical point qq of f1f_{1} we set

ℳ~qp={γ:ℝ\displaystyle\widetilde{\mathcal{M}}^{p}_{q}=\{\gamma\colon\thinspace\mathbb{R} →M​ such that\displaystyle\to M\text{ such that}
1) γ is a flow line of V,\displaystyle\text{1) $\gamma$ is a flow line of $V$},
2) limt→−∞γ​(t)=p,and\displaystyle\text{2) $\lim_{t\to-\infty}\gamma(t)=p$},\text{and}
3) limt→∞γ​(t)=q}.\displaystyle\text{3) $\lim_{t\to\infty}\gamma(t)=q$}\}.

We note that since VV is a time-dependent vector field, there is no ℝ\mathbb{R} action on ℳqp~\widetilde{\mathcal{M}^{p}_{q}} as there was above. We now have analogs of Morse Theorems 1 through 4.

We note that there is no −1-1 as in Morse Theorem 1 since there is no ℝ\mathbb{R} action on flow lines of the time-dependent vector field VV.

The meaning of “consistently” is similar to its meaning above. In particular, it means the orientations in the gluing theorem below are consistent. We now move to the compactness theorem.

The notion of convergence is similar to the one above, but slightly different. We say that {γi}\{\gamma_{i}\} converges to a broken flow line if there are critical points p=p1,p2,…,pkp=p_{1},p_{2},\ldots,p_{k} of f0f_{0} and critical points pk+1,…,pl=qp_{k+1},\ldots,p_{l}=q of f1f_{1} such that the indices are strictly decreasing except for possibly pkp_{k} and pk+1p_{k+1} which can have the same index, and elements γ~j∈ℳ​(f0)pj+1pj\widetilde{\gamma}_{j}\in\mathcal{M}(f_{0})^{p_{j}}_{p_{j+1}} for j<kj<k, γ~j∈ℳ​(f1)pj+1pj\widetilde{\gamma}_{j}\in\mathcal{M}(f_{1})^{p_{j}}_{p_{j+1}} for j>kj>k, and γ~k∈ℳ~pk+1pk\widetilde{\gamma}_{k}\in\widetilde{\mathcal{M}}^{p_{k}}_{p_{k+1}} such that the γi\gamma_{i} converge to {γ~j}j=1l\{\widetilde{\gamma}_{j}\}_{j=1}^{l} as in Morse Theorem 3.

We now turn to the “gluing” theorem, but state it a bit vaguely and leave it to the reader to see how to adopt the statement of Morse Theorem 4 to the current situation.

We now have the immediate corollaries whose proofs are very similar to the analogous ones in the previous section.

Corollary 2.28.

If pp is a critical point of f0f_{0}, qq is a critical point of f1f_{1} such that index⁡p=index⁡q\operatorname{index}p=\operatorname{index}q, then ℳ~qp\widetilde{\mathcal{M}}^{p}_{q} is a compact 0-manifold.

Corollary 2.29.

If pp is a critical point of f0f_{0}, qq is a critical point of f1f_{1} such that k=index⁡p=index⁡q+1k=\operatorname{index}p=\operatorname{index}q+1, then ℳ~¯qp\overline{\widetilde{\mathcal{M}}}^{p}_{q} is a compact 11-manifold with boundary

(⋃r∈Critk−1​(f0)(ℳrp×ℳ~qr))​⋃(⋃r∈Critk​(f1)(ℳ~rp×ℳqr))\left(\bigcup_{r\in\text{Crit}_{k-1}(f_{0})}\left(\mathcal{M}^{p}_{r}\times\widetilde{\mathcal{M}}^{r}_{q}\right)\right)\bigcup\left(\bigcup_{r\in\text{Crit}_{k}(f_{1})}\left(\widetilde{\mathcal{M}}^{p}_{r}\times\mathcal{M}^{r}_{q}\right)\right)

where Critk​(f)\text{Crit}_{k}(f) is the set of critical points of ff with index kk.

We can now define Φ:Cn0→Cn1\Phi\colon\thinspace C_{n}^{0}\to C_{n}^{1} on a generator p∈Cn0p\in C^{0}_{n} by

ΦΓ​(p)=∑q∈Critn​(f1)|ℳ~qp|​q.\Phi_{\Gamma}(p)=\sum_{q\in\text{Crit}_{n}(f_{1})}|\widetilde{\mathcal{M}}^{p}_{q}|q.

Where, again, |ℳ~qp||\widetilde{\mathcal{M}}^{p}_{q}| means the signed count of points in ℳ~qp\widetilde{\mathcal{M}}^{p}_{q}. Clearly, Morse Theorems 1’ through 3’ show ΦΓ\Phi_{\Gamma} is a well-defined homomorphism. We now see that this is a chain map.

Theorem 2.30.

With the notation above

ΦΓ∘∂0+∂1∘ΦΓ=0.\Phi_{\Gamma}\circ\partial_{0}+\partial_{1}\circ\Phi_{\Gamma}=0.

From basic homological algebra, we have the immediate corollary.

Corollary 2.31.

The map ΦΓ\Phi_{\Gamma} induces a homomorphism

H​(C0,∂0)→H​(C1,∂1),H(C^{0},\partial^{0})\to H(C^{1},\partial^{1}),

where H​(Ci,∂i)H(C^{i},\partial^{i}) is the homology of (Ci,∂i)(C^{i},\partial^{i}).

Proof of Theorem 2.30.

We will denote by Critk​(fi)\text{Crit}_{k}(f_{i}) the critical points of fif_{i} with index kk. Now take a critical point pp of f0f_{0} with index nn. Then we have

(ΦΓ∘∂0)​(p)\displaystyle(\Phi_{\Gamma}\circ\partial_{0})(p) +(∂1∘ΦΓ)​(p)=ΦΓ​(∑r∈Critn−1​(f0)|ℳrp|​r)+∂1(∑r∈Critn​(f1)|ℳ~rp|​r)\displaystyle+(\partial_{1}\circ\Phi_{\Gamma})(p)=\Phi_{\Gamma}\left(\sum_{r\in\text{Crit}_{n-1}(f_{0})}|\mathcal{M}^{p}_{r}|r\right)+\partial_{1}\left(\sum_{r\in\text{Crit}_{n}(f_{1})}|\widetilde{\mathcal{M}}^{p}_{r}|r\right)
=∑r∈Critn−1​(f0)(|ℳrp|​∑q∈Critn−1​(f1)|ℳ~qr|​q)+∑r∈Critn​(f1)(|ℳ~rp|​∑q∈Critn−1​(f1)|ℳqr|​q)\displaystyle=\sum_{r\in\text{Crit}_{n-1}(f_{0})}\left(|\mathcal{M}^{p}_{r}|\sum_{q\in\text{Crit}_{n-1}(f_{1})}|\widetilde{\mathcal{M}}^{r}_{q}|q\right)+\sum_{r\in\text{Crit}_{n}(f_{1})}\left(|\widetilde{\mathcal{M}}^{p}_{r}|\sum_{q\in\text{Crit}_{n-1}(f_{1})}|\mathcal{M}^{r}_{q}|q\right)
=∑q∈Critn−1​(f0)(∑r∈Critn−1​(f0)|ℳrp|​|ℳ~qr|+∑r∈Critn​(f1)|ℳ~rp|​|ℳqr|)​q\displaystyle=\sum_{q\in\text{Crit}_{n-1}(f_{0})}\left(\sum_{r\in\text{Crit}_{n-1}(f_{0})}|\mathcal{M}^{p}_{r}||\widetilde{\mathcal{M}}^{r}_{q}|+\sum_{r\in\text{Crit}_{n}(f_{1})}|\widetilde{\mathcal{M}}^{p}_{r}||\mathcal{M}^{r}_{q}|\right)q
=∑q∈Critn−1​(f0)|∂ℳ~qp¯|​q=0,\displaystyle=\sum_{q\in\text{Crit}_{n-1}(f_{0})}|\partial\overline{\widetilde{\mathcal{M}}^{p}_{q}}|q=0,

since ℳ~qp¯\overline{\widetilde{\mathcal{M}}^{p}_{q}} is a compact 11-manifold with boundary. ∎

In Figure 9 we see the “picture proof” of this theorem. Here p∈Critn​(f0),q∈Critn−1​(f1)p\in\text{Crit}_{n}(f_{0}),q\in\text{Crit}_{n-1}(f_{1}) and rr and r′r^{\prime} are either in Critn​(f1)\text{Crit}_{n}(f_{1}) or Critn−1​(f0)\text{Crit}_{n-1}(f_{0}).

So we see the terms showing up in (ΦΓ∘∂0+∂1∘ΦΓ)​(p)(\Phi_{\Gamma}\circ\partial_{0}+\partial_{1}\circ\Phi_{\Gamma})(p) are simply the boundary of ℳ~qp¯\overline{\widetilde{\mathcal{M}}^{p}_{q}}.

We would now like to see that ΦΓ\Phi_{\Gamma} induces an isomorphism. To achieve this we consider two paths Γ0\Gamma_{0} and Γ1\Gamma_{1} from (f0,g0)(f_{0},g_{0}) to (f1,g1)(f_{1},g_{1}) and let DD be a path from Γ0\Gamma_{0} to Γ1\Gamma_{1}. That is,

D={(fs,t,gs,t) for (s,t)∈[0,1]×ℝ and Γ0=(f0,t,g0,t),Γ1=(f1,t,g1,t)}.D=\{(f_{s,t},g_{s,t})\text{ for }(s,t)\in[0,1]\times\mathbb{R}\text{ and }\Gamma_{0}=(f_{0,t},g_{0,t}),\Gamma_{1}=(f_{1,t},g_{1,t})\}.

Now if p∈Crit​(f0)p\in\text{Crit}(f_{0}) and q∈Crit​(f1)q\in\text{Crit}(f_{1}) then we set

ℳ^qp={(γ,s):γ∈ℳ~qp​(s)},\widehat{\mathcal{M}}^{p}_{q}=\{(\gamma,s):\gamma\in\widetilde{\mathcal{M}}^{p}_{q}(s)\},

where ℳ~qp​(s)\widetilde{\mathcal{M}}^{p}_{q}(s) is ℳ~qp\widetilde{\mathcal{M}}^{p}_{q} for the path Γs=(fs,t,gs,t)\Gamma_{s}=(f_{s,t},g_{s,t}). We have the “same” Morse Theorems 1 through 4 for ℳ^qp\widehat{\mathcal{M}}^{p}_{q} except that

dimℳ^qp=index⁡p−index⁡q+1\dim\widehat{\mathcal{M}}^{p}_{q}=\operatorname{index}p-\operatorname{index}q+1

and the “obvious” changes to the compactness and gluing theorems.

Exercise 2.32.

Figure out what the “obvious” changes are.

Now we can define K:Cn0→Cn+11K\colon\thinspace C_{n}^{0}\to C_{n+1}^{1} by

K​(p)=∑q∈Critn+1​(f1)|ℳ^qp|​q.K(p)=\sum_{q\in\text{Crit}_{n+1}(f_{1})}|\widehat{\mathcal{M}}^{p}_{q}|q.

This gives us a chain homotopy equivalence between ΦΓ0\Phi_{\Gamma_{0}} and Φγ1\Phi_{\gamma_{1}}.

Theorem 2.33.

With the notation above

∂1∘K+K∘∂0+ΦΓ0+ΦΓ1=0.\partial_{1}\circ K+K\circ\partial_{0}+\Phi_{\Gamma_{0}}+\Phi_{\Gamma_{1}}=0.
Exercise 2.34.

Using the previous exercise, prove this theorem.
Hint: Just as in the previous theorems, notice that the count of points in ∂ℳ^qp¯\partial\overline{\widehat{\mathcal{M}}^{p}_{q}} is exactly the coefficient of qq in the application of the left-hand side of the formula in the theorem to pp.

Of course, standard homological algebra tells us that the previous theorem shows ΦΓ0\Phi_{\Gamma_{0}} and ΦΓ1\Phi_{\Gamma_{1}} induce the same map on homology (or the way it is stated, one map is minus the other).

Corollary 2.35.

With the notation above the maps ΦΓ0\Phi_{\Gamma_{0}} and −ΦΓ1-\Phi_{\Gamma_{1}} induce the same maps on homology.

To complete our proof that the Morse homology is independent of the choices of Morse function and Riemannian metric, we have the following exercises.

Exercise 2.36.

Let Γ\Gamma be the constant path from (f,g)(f,g) to (f,g)(f,g). Show that ΦΓ\Phi_{\Gamma} induces the identity map on homology.
Hint: Of course, one must perturb Γ\Gamma so that it is “generic” and Theorems 1′1^{\prime} through 4′4^{\prime} hold, but this perturbation can be chosen to be arbitrarily small.

Exercise 2.37.

If Γ1\Gamma_{1} is a path from (f0,g0)(f_{0},g_{0}) to (f1,g1)(f_{1},g_{1}) and Γ2\Gamma_{2} is a path from (f1,g1)(f_{1},g_{1}) to (f2,g2)(f_{2},g_{2}), then we can define the concatenated path Γ\Gamma that agrees with Γ1\Gamma_{1} for t≤1t\leq 1 and agrees with Γ2\Gamma_{2}, shifted by 22, for t≥1t\geq 1. Show that ΦΓ2∘ΦΓ1=ΦΓ\Phi_{\Gamma_{2}}\circ\Phi_{\Gamma_{1}}=\Phi_{\Gamma} on homology.

Theorem 2.38.

The Morse homology of MM is independent of the Morse function ff and Riemannian metric gg.

Exercise 2.39.

Use the above exercises and theorems to prove this.

2.5. A few details of the main theorems

In this section, we will give an indication of how to prove Morse Theorems 11 through 44 (and hence 1′1^{\prime} through 4′4^{\prime} too). We will not give complete proofs as they are quite intricate and involve a lot of analysis that can obscure the main ideas on a first pass at the subject. One may find more details in [25, 31, 42]; in particular, the presenation here was partially inspired by the second listed reference. We also note that one can define Morse homology with chain groups being ℤ/2​ℤ\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z} vector spaces generated by critical points, and in this case, one does not need Morse Theorem 22 about the orientability of the spaces ℳqp\mathcal{M}^{p}_{q}. In many situations, it is common for people to work over ℤ/2​ℤ\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z} and ignore this theorem. We will do that here and only discuss the other theorems.

We will not describe the ideas of the proof of the main theorems for the other homology theories, but they mostly follow the same general idea (though details are quite different), except for the proof of the compactness result, which are quite different.

2.5.1. Morse Theorem 11

Given a Morse function f:M→ℝf\colon\thinspace M\to\mathbb{R} and a Riemannian metric gg on MM we define the path space between two points pp and qq to be

𝒫qp={γ:ℝ→M:limt→−∞γ​(t)=p​ and ​limt→∞γ​(t)=q}.\mathcal{P}^{p}_{q}=\{\gamma\colon\thinspace\mathbb{R}\to M:\lim_{t\to-\infty}\gamma(t)=p\text{ and }\lim_{t\to\infty}\gamma(t)=q\}.

We will need to know what the tangent space of 𝒫qp\mathcal{P}^{p}_{q} is. (At this point, we are ignoring issues of smoothness. That is, we are not specifying if γ\gamma is smooth or CkC^{k} or something else. We will discuss this later.) To this end, recall that one may think of a tangent vector at a point as the equivalence class of paths through that point that have the same first derivative at that point. So if γ∈𝒫qp\gamma\in\mathcal{P}^{p}_{q}, then we can consider a path s→γss\to\gamma_{s} for s∈(−ϵ,ϵ)s\in(-\epsilon,\epsilon) with γ0=γ\gamma_{0}=\gamma. Notice that γs​(t0)\gamma_{s}(t_{0}) for a fixed t0t_{0} is a path in MM such that γ0​(t0)\gamma_{0}(t_{0}) is γ​(t0)\gamma(t_{0}). Thus

v​(t0)=∂γs​(t0)∂s|s=0v(t_{0})=\frac{\partial\gamma_{s}(t_{0})}{\partial s}\Big|_{s=0}

is a vector in Tγ​(t0)​MT_{\gamma(t_{0})}M. See Figure 10.

So v​(t)v(t) for t∈ℝt\in\mathbb{R} is a vector field along γ\gamma, by this we mean that for each γ​(t)\gamma(t) the vector v​(t)∈Tγ​(t)​Mv(t)\in T_{\gamma(t)}M. Summing up, given a path in 𝒫qp\mathcal{P}^{p}_{q} through γ\gamma its “derivative” at γ​(t)\gamma(t) is a vector field v​(t)v(t) along γ\gamma. We also know that since γs∈𝒫qp\gamma_{s}\in\mathcal{P}^{p}_{q} we must have that v​(t)→0v(t)\to 0 as t→±∞t\to\pm\infty.

We can easily see the converse too. Given any vector field v​(t)v(t) along γ\gamma that decays to 0 as tt approaches ±∞\pm\infty, extend vv to a vector field VV on all of MM. Let Γ:M×ℝ→M\Gamma\colon\thinspace M\times\mathbb{R}\to M be the flow of VV. We can now define

γs​(t)=Γ​(γ​(t),s).\gamma_{s}(t)=\Gamma(\gamma(t),s).

This gives a path in 𝒫qp\mathcal{P}^{p}_{q} through γ\gamma and by the properties of the flow of a vector field, we see that the vector field associated to γs\gamma_{s} along γ\gamma is simply vv. Thus, vectors tangent to 𝒫qp\mathcal{P}^{p}_{q} at γ\gamma are in one-to-one correspondence with vector fields along γ\gamma that decay to 0 as tt approaches ±∞\pm\infty. So we see

Tγ​(𝒫qp)\displaystyle T_{\gamma}(\mathcal{P}^{p}_{q}) ={vector fields along γ that decay to 0 as t approaches ±∞}\displaystyle=\{\text{vector fields along $\gamma$ that decay to $0$ as $t$ approaches $\pm\infty$}\}
=Γd​(γ∗​T​M)\displaystyle=\Gamma_{d}(\gamma^{*}TM)

where γ∗​T​M\gamma^{*}TM is the pull-back of T​MTM to ℝ\mathbb{R} by γ\gamma and Γd\Gamma_{d} denotes sections of this bundle that decay as indicated.

We now suppose that pp and qq are critical points of ff and consider the map

S:𝒫qp→T​𝒫qp:γ↦γ′​(t)+∇fγ​(t),S\colon\thinspace\mathcal{P}^{p}_{q}\to T\mathcal{P}^{p}_{q}\colon\thinspace\gamma\mapsto\gamma^{\prime}(t)+\nabla f_{\gamma(t)},

where ∇f\nabla f is the gradient of ff with respect to gg. Clearly γ′​(t)+∇fγ​(t)\gamma^{\prime}(t)+\nabla f_{\gamma(t)} is an element of Γd​(γ∗​T​M)\Gamma_{d}(\gamma^{*}TM) as desired and also that the space of parameterized flow lines, which we denote by ℳ¯qp\underline{\mathcal{M}}^{p}_{q}, is

ℳ¯qp=S−1​(Z)\underline{\mathcal{M}}^{p}_{q}=S^{-1}(Z)

where ZZ is the zero section of T​𝒫qpT\mathcal{P}^{p}_{q}. We note that ℝ\mathbb{R} acts on ℳ¯qp\underline{\mathcal{M}}^{p}_{q} freely by reparameterization and thus if ℳ¯qp\underline{\mathcal{M}}^{p}_{q} is a manifold then so is ℳqp{\mathcal{M}}^{p}_{q}. So we have managed to describe the space we are interested in, that is ℳqp\mathcal{M}^{p}_{q}, as the preimage of a submanifold ZZ under some function SS (after modding out by reparameterization). Thus, from standard differential topology, if SS were “transverse” to ZZ then we would expect ℳ¯qp\underline{\mathcal{M}}^{p}_{q} to be a manifold. Of course, the problem here is that 𝒫qp\mathcal{P}^{p}_{q} and T​𝒫qpT\mathcal{P}^{p}_{q} are infinite-dimensional, and a standard differential topology course does not deal with these. So we will take a brief digression to address this and then return to a sketch of the proof that ℳqp\mathcal{M}^{p}_{q} is a manifold.

2.5.2. Recollections on transversality and infinite dimensional manifolds

We begin by recalling the “standard way” to create functions transverse to submanifolds in finite dimensions. Given a smooth function f:X→Yf\colon\thinspace X\to Y between manifolds and a submanifold ZZ of YY we would like to “make” ff transverse to ZZ, by which we mean we would like to find arbitrarily small perturbations of ff so that it is transverse to ZZ (if it is not already transverse). The idea is to try to choose a perturbation space PP and extend ff to a map F:X×P→YF\colon\thinspace X\times P\to Y. Since PP adds dimensions to the domain, FF has a better chance of being transverse to ZZ (and of course, for the right space PP, it will be). We then use the following standard theorem from differential topology.

Theorem 2.40.

With the notation in the previous paragraph, assume that FF is transverse to ZZ and let π:X×P→P\pi\colon\thinspace X\times P\to P be the projection map. If pp is a regular value of π|F−1​(Z):F−1​(Z)→P\pi|_{F^{-1}(Z)}\colon\thinspace F^{-1}(Z)\to P, then fp​(⋅)=F​(⋅,p):X→Yf_{p}(\cdot)=F(\cdot,p)\colon\thinspace X\to Y is transverse to ZZ.

Exercise 2.41.

Prove this theorem for X,Y,X,Y, and PP of arbitrary dimension.

In finite dimensions have we have Sard’s Theorem, which tells us when there is a dense set of regular values pp.

Theorem 2.42 (Sard’s Theorem).

If h:M→Nh\colon\thinspace M\to N is a CkC^{k}-map then the set of regular values of hh is dense in NN if k>max⁡{0,m−n}k>\max\{0,m-n\}.

Thus returning to our original problem stated above, in the finite dimensional setting, if FF is smooth enough, then there will be fp:X→Yf_{p}\colon\thinspace X\to Y transverse to ZZ for a dense set of pp and thus ff may be “approximated” by a map transverse to ZZ (once we find FF!).

The infinite-dimensional setting is a bit more complicated. For this, we begin with a few definitions. First, recall that a linear space VV with a norm ∥⋅∥:V→ℝ\|\cdot\|\colon\thinspace V\to\mathbb{R} is a Banach space if any Cauchy sequence in VV (that is sequence viv_{i} such that for any ϵ>0\epsilon>0 there is some NN such that ‖vi−vj‖<ϵ\|v_{i}-v_{j}\|<\epsilon for all i,j>Ni,j>N) converges to a point in VV. Given two Banach spaces VV and WW (we will suppress the norms, though they are essential) we say a bounded linear map L:V→WL\colon\thinspace V\to W is a Fredholm operator if

  • •

    LL has a closed image,

  • •

    the dimension of ker⁡F\ker F is finite, and

  • •

    the dimension of cokerF\rm{coker}F is finite,

where the cokerF\rm{coker}F is simply W/F​(V)W/F(V) and is called the co-kernel. We define the index of a Fredholm operator to be

index⁡F=dimker⁡(F)−dimcoker​(F).\operatorname{index}F=\dim\ker(F)-\dim\rm{coker}(F).

Finally, a Banach manifold MM is a topological manifold with coordinate charts modeled on a Banach space (V,∥⋅∥)(V,\|\cdot\|) and having smooth transition maps. We can now generalize Sard’s theorem.

Theorem 2.43 (Sard-Smale).

Let XX and YY be separable Banach manifolds and h:X→Yh\colon\thinspace X\to Y be a CkC^{k}-map such that

d​hx:Tx​X→Tf​(x)​Ydh_{x}\colon\thinspace T_{x}X\to T_{f(x)}Y

is Fredholm of index ll for all x∈Xx\in X. If k≥max⁡{1,l+1}k\geq\max\{1,l+1\}, then the regular values of hh are dense in YY. (And for such a yy, h−1​(y)h^{-1}(y) is a manifold of dimension ll.)

With this theorem and an improved version of Theorem 2.40 we have the following result that will be useful to prove ℳqp\mathcal{M}^{p}_{q} is a manifold. We first set up some notation. Let XX and PP be Banach manifolds, and π:E→X×P\pi\colon\thinspace E\to X\times P be a Banach vector bundle (the reader should think through what this might mean). Suppose ϕ:(X×P)→E\phi\colon\thinspace(X\times P)\to E is a section and ZZ is the zero section of EE. We want to discuss when ϕ\phi is transverse to ZZ. Note that ker⁡d​πe:Te​E→Tϕ​(e)​(X×P)\ker d\pi_{e}\colon\thinspace T_{e}E\to T_{\phi(e)}(X\times P) is naturally identified with the fiber Eϕ​(e)E_{\phi(e)} of EE above ϕ​(e)\phi(e), since the tangent space to a linear space is naturally the linear space. So Te​E=ker⁡d​πe⊕HxT_{e}E=\ker d\pi_{e}\oplus H_{x} where HeH_{e} is a “horizontal” subspace of Te​ET_{e}E; that is, it is transverse to ker⁡d​πe\ker d\pi_{e} and its intersection with ker⁡d​πe\ker d\pi_{e} is {0}\{0\}. In general HeH_{e} is determined by a “connection” on the bundle EE but if e∈Ze\in Z then we can take He=Te​ZH_{e}=T_{e}Z, so there is no need for a connection. We can now define ∇ϕ​(e)ϕ\nabla_{\phi(e)}\phi to be the composition of d​ϕϕ​(e)d\phi_{\phi(e)} with the projection to Eϕ​(e)E_{\phi(e)} using the splitting Te​E=Eϕ​(e)⊕Te​ZT_{e}E=E_{\phi(e)}\oplus T_{e}Z.

Theorem 2.44.

With the notation above, suppose ϕ:(X×P)→E\phi\colon\thinspace(X\times P)\to E is a section of the bundle and for all (x,p)∈ϕ−1​(Z)(x,p)\in\phi^{-1}(Z), we have

  1. (1)

    ∇(x,p)ϕ:T(x,p)​(X×P)→Tϕ​(x,p)​π−1​((x,p))\nabla_{(x,p)}\phi\colon\thinspace T_{(x,p)}(X\times P)\to T_{\phi(x,p)}\pi^{-1}((x,p)) is surjective, and

  2. (2)

    if ϕp\phi_{p} is the map ϕp:X→E:x↦ϕ​(x,p)\phi_{p}\colon\thinspace X\to E\colon\thinspace x\mapsto\phi(x,p) then ∇(x,p)ϕp:Tx​X→Tϕ​(x,p)​π−1​((x,p))\nabla_{(x,p)}\phi_{p}\colon\thinspace T_{x}X\to T_{\phi(x,p)}\pi^{-1}((x,p)) is Fredholm of index ll.

Then, for a generic p∈Pp\in P we have ϕp−1​(Z)\phi_{p}^{-1}(Z) is a manifold of dimension ll.

We can finally sketch the proof of Morse Theorem 11.

Sketch of the proof of Morse Theorem 11.

Recall we have the space 𝒫qp\mathcal{P}^{p}_{q} of paths between critical points pp and qq of a Morse function ff, and its tangent space at γ\gamma is Γd​(γ∗​T​M)\Gamma_{d}(\gamma^{*}TM). Trying to apply the theorems above, we choose our space of perturbations to be the set PP of all Riemannian metrics on MM. Now let EE be the bundle over 𝒫qp×P\mathcal{P}^{p}_{q}\times P with fiber at (γ,g)(\gamma,g) the space Γd​(γ∗​T​M)\Gamma_{d}(\gamma^{*}TM). We can now define the section

ϕ:(𝒫qp×P)→E:(γ,g)↦γ′​(t)+V​(γ​(t))\phi\colon\thinspace(\mathcal{P}^{p}_{q}\times P)\to E\colon\thinspace(\gamma,g)\mapsto\gamma^{\prime}(t)+V(\gamma(t))

where V=∇gfV=\nabla_{g}f is the gradient of ff with respect to gg.

Exercise 2.45.

Show that ϕ​(γ,g)\phi(\gamma,g) is the zero vector field along γ\gamma if and only if γ\gamma is a gradient flow line of ∇gf\nabla_{g}f from pp to qq.

Now, if we can find the appropriate function spaces for 𝒫qp\mathcal{P}^{p}_{q}, PP, and EE, such that ϕ\phi is Fredholm and transverse to the zero section in EE, then there would be a dense set of gg for which the Moduli space ℳ¯qp\underline{\mathcal{M}}^{p}_{q} is a manifold. Moreover, if we can compute that the index of ϕ\phi is index⁡p−index⁡q\operatorname{index}p-\operatorname{index}q then it would also have the desired dimensions.

For example, for PP one could consider the space of CkC^{k}-smooth Riemannian metrics on MM, and for the other spaces one could choose appropriate Sobolev spaces where some number of derivatives have bounded L2L^{2}-integrals. To carry out the details would be a long diversion for these lecture notes, so we refer the reader to [31], but we do briefly indicate why ϕ\phi will be transverse to the zero section in the right functional analytic setup.

Given a tangent vector (γ~,g~)(\widetilde{\gamma},\widetilde{g}) in T(γ,g)​(𝒫qp×P)T_{(\gamma,g)}(\mathcal{P}^{p}_{q}\times P) we can represent it by a path (γt,gt)(\gamma_{t},g_{t}) such that (γ0,g0)=(γ,g)(\gamma_{0},g_{0})=(\gamma,g) and (γ˙t|t=0,g˙t|t=0)=(γ~,g~)(\dot{\gamma}_{t}|_{t=0},\dot{g}_{t}|_{t=0})=(\widetilde{\gamma},\widetilde{g}) where the dot represents the derivative with respect to the tt parameter. Now

ϕ​(γt,gt)​(s)=γt′​(s)+Vγt​(s)t\phi(\gamma_{t},g_{t})(s)=\gamma^{\prime}_{t}(s)+V^{t}_{\gamma_{t}(s)}

where VtV^{t} is the gradient of ff with respect to gtg_{t}, and the prime denotes the derivative with respect to ss. Now

d​ϕ(γ,g)​(γ~,g~)\displaystyle d\phi_{(\gamma,g)}(\widetilde{\gamma},\widetilde{g}) =dd​t​ϕ​(γt,gt)|t=0\displaystyle=\frac{d}{dt}\phi(\gamma_{t},g_{t})|_{t=0}
=dd​t​(dd​s​γt​(s))|t=0+dd​t​Vγt​(s)0|t=0+dd​t​Vγ0​(s)t|t=0.\displaystyle=\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{d}{ds}\gamma_{t}(s)\right)\big|_{t=0}+\frac{d}{dt}V^{0}_{\gamma_{t}(s)}\big|_{t=0}+\frac{d}{dt}V^{t}_{\gamma_{0}(s)}\big|_{t=0}.

If we choose our path so that γt=γ\gamma_{t}=\gamma for all tt, then we see that

d​ϕ(γ,g)​(γ~,g~)=dd​t​Vγ​(s)t|t=0.d\phi_{(\gamma,g)}(\widetilde{\gamma},\widetilde{g})=\frac{d}{dt}V^{t}_{\gamma(s)}\big|_{t=0}.

We let V˙\dot{V} be the projection of dd​t​Vγ​(s)t|t=0\frac{d}{dt}V^{t}_{\gamma(s)}\big|_{t=0} to the fiber of the bundle EE (that is to Γd​(γ∗​T​M)\Gamma_{d}(\gamma^{*}TM)). To work out V˙\dot{V}, we recall that VtV^{t} is the gradient of ff with respect to gtg_{t}. If we choose local coordinates x1,…,xnx_{1},\ldots,x_{n}, then we can write

gt=∑i,j=1n(gt)i​j​d​xi⊗d​xjg_{t}=\sum_{i,j=1}^{n}(g_{t})_{ij}dx^{i}\otimes dx^{j}

for some real valued functions (gt)i​j(g_{t})_{ij}. Thinking of ((gt)i​j)\begin{pmatrix}(g_{t})_{ij}\end{pmatrix} as a matrix we let ((gt)i​j)\begin{pmatrix}(g_{t})^{ij}\end{pmatrix} be its inverse and since

d​f=∑i=1n∂f∂xi​d​xidf=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_{i}}\,dx_{i}

we see that

Vt=∑i,j=1n(gt)i​j|t=0​∂f∂xi​dd​xj.V^{t}=\sum_{i,j=1}^{n}(g_{t})^{ij}\big|_{t=0}\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_{i}}\frac{d}{dx_{j}}.

Thus

V˙=∑i,j=1ndd​t​(gt)i​j|t=0​∂f∂xi​dd​xj.\dot{V}=\sum_{i,j=1}^{n}\frac{d}{dt}(g_{t})^{ij}\big|_{t=0}\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_{i}}\frac{d}{dx_{j}}.

We now suppose that vv is a vector field along γ\gamma in Γd​(γ∗​T​M)\Gamma_{d}(\gamma^{*}TM). In local coordiates we can write it ∑i=1nai​dd​xi\sum_{i=1}^{n}a_{i}\,\frac{d}{dx_{i}}. Let AA be the matrix

(a1a2⋯ana20⋯0⋮⋱an0⋯0)\begin{pmatrix}a_{1}&a_{2}&\cdots&a_{n}\\ a_{2}&0&\cdots&0\\ \vdots&&\ddots&\\ a_{n}&0&\cdots&0\end{pmatrix}

with vv in the first column and first row and 0’s elsewhere. Now if gg is given by the matrix G=(gi​j)G=\begin{pmatrix}g_{ij}\end{pmatrix} then define the matrix Gt−1=G−1+t​AG_{t}^{-1}=G^{-1}+tA and GtG_{t} as its inverse. For tt small, the inverse will exist and define a Riemannian metric gtg_{t}. We can further choose coordinates near some point on γ\gamma so that ∇f=(10⋯0)T\nabla f=\begin{pmatrix}1&0&\cdots&0\end{pmatrix}^{T}. Thus, we see that near this point on γ\gamma the vector V˙=v\dot{V}=v. Using a cutoff function, we can find a global family of metrics gtg_{t} on MM so that near the desired point we still have V˙=v\dot{V}=v. That is, locally any element of Γd​(γ∗​T​M)\Gamma_{d}(\gamma^{*}TM) is in the image of ∇(γ,g)ϕ\nabla_{(\gamma,g)}\phi.

In the appropriate functional analytic setting, this implies that ∇(γ,g)ϕ\nabla_{(\gamma,g)}\phi is onto Eγ,g=Γd​(γ∗​T​M)E_{\gamma,g}=\Gamma_{d}(\gamma^{*}TM). We sketch some details here. To this end, we need to choose a Banach structure on the bundle EE. Given a fixed metric on MM and we can define the L2L^{2}-innner product on elements f,g∈Γd​(γ∗​T​M)f,g\in\Gamma_{d}(\gamma^{*}TM) to be

⟨f,g⟩=∫ℝ⟨f​(s),g​(s)⟩​𝑑s,\langle f,g\rangle=\int_{\mathbb{R}}\langle f(s),g(s)\rangle\,ds,

where the inner product in the integrand is coming from the metric on MM. This gives a norm ‖f‖2=⟨f,f⟩\|f\|^{2}=\langle f,f\rangle and hence a metric Γd​(γ∗​T​M)\Gamma_{d}(\gamma^{*}TM) thought of as smooth sections. We can now complete this space with respect to this metric to obtain Ld2​(γ∗​T​M)L^{2}_{d}(\gamma^{*}TM), the space of L2L^{2}-integrable sections of γ∗​T​M\gamma^{*}TM.

We would now like to show that the orthogonal complement (with respect to the L2L^{2}-inner product) of the image of d​ϕ(γ,g)d\phi_{(\gamma,g)} in Ld2​(γ∗​T​M)L^{2}_{d}(\gamma^{*}TM) is the trivial vector space {0}\{0\}. So let ω\omega be an element of Ld2​(γ∗​T​M)L^{2}_{d}(\gamma^{*}TM) orthogonal to the image of d​ϕ(γ,g)d\phi_{(\gamma,g)}. Recall that d​ϕ(γ,g)​(0,g~)=V˙d\phi_{(\gamma,g)}(0,\widetilde{g})=\dot{V}, where V˙\dot{V} is defined above. We will show that anything orthogonal to V˙\dot{V} for all V˙\dot{V} is actually zero (notice that this shows that a vector even being orthogonal to a subset of image⁡(d​ϕ(γ,g))\operatorname{image}(d\phi_{(\gamma,g)}) is enough to show it is zero). So we assume that, for any V˙\dot{V}, we have

∫γ⟨V˙,ω⟩​𝑑s=0.\int_{\gamma}\langle\dot{V},\omega\rangle\,ds=0.

Now, for any x∈ℝx\in\mathbb{R}, we can choose a neighborhood NN of γ​(x)\gamma(x) so that N∩image⁡(γ)N\cap\operatorname{image}(\gamma) is the image of an interval II. Choose a function h:ℝ→ℝh\colon\thinspace\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R} so that h=1h=1 near xx, h=0h=0 oustide II, and h≥0h\geq 0. Now set v=h​ωv=h\omega. From above, we can choose g~\widetilde{g} so that V˙=v\dot{V}=v where hh is non-zero. We now compute

0=∫ℝ⟨V˙,ω⟩​𝑑s=∫ℝh​⟨ω,ω⟩​𝑑s.0=\int_{\mathbb{R}}\langle\dot{V},\omega\rangle\,ds=\int_{\mathbb{R}}h\langle\omega,\omega\rangle\,ds.

This implies that h​‖ω‖2=0h\|\omega\|^{2}=0 and so ‖ω‖=0\|\omega\|=0 near xx. But since xx was arbitrary ω=0\omega=0, as we wanted to prove. Note, there are details here involving the Banach structure we use on the domain and the continuity (or lack thereof) of ω\omega, but we leave it to the reader and the references above to sort out these details. The main point was to give an indication of the flavor of the argument necessary to prove some sort of “transversallity” result.

In a similar fashion, but with more work, one may also show that ∇(x,p)ϕ\nabla_{(x,p)}\phi for a fixed gg satisfies the conditions in Theorem 2.44, which gives us Morse Theorem  11. ∎

2.5.3. Morse Theorem 33

Given a Morse function f:M→ℝf\colon\thinspace M\to\mathbb{R} we choose a Riemannian metric gg so that Morse Theorem 11 holds. We want to consider a sequence of flow lines from the critical point pp to the critical point qq. Let γi\gamma_{i} be such a sequence and assume it is a Cauchy sequence, so it should converge. To analyze this situation, we note that one can choose a small ball B1B_{1} about pp so that B1B_{1} contains no other critical points and any gradient flow line from pp transversely intersects ∂B1\partial B_{1} in one point.

Exercise 2.46.

Use the Morse Lemma, Theorem 2.7, to prove that such a ball exists.

Let xix_{i} be the unique point at which γi\gamma_{i} intersects ∂B1\partial B_{1}. One may easily check that {xi}\{x_{i}\} is a Cauchy sequence in the compact set ∂B\partial B and so it converges to a point x∞x_{\infty}. See the upper left diagram in Figure 11. Now let γ~1\widetilde{\gamma}_{1} be the gradient flow line through x∞x_{\infty}. If γ~1\widetilde{\gamma}_{1} flows to qq then the γi\gamma_{i} converge to an element of ℳqp\mathcal{M}^{p}_{q}. We now need to see that the γi\gamma_{i} converge to a broken flow line.

So suppose limt→∞γ~1​(t)\lim_{t\to\infty}\widetilde{\gamma}_{1}(t) is the critical point r2≠qr_{2}\not=q (recall, we are taking r1=pr_{1}=p). Since ℳr2p\mathcal{M}^{p}_{r_{2}} is non-empty, we know its dimension is greater than or equal to 0 and so index⁡r2<index⁡p\operatorname{index}r_{2}<\operatorname{index}p. We can parameterize the γi\gamma_{i} so that γi​(0)=xi\gamma_{i}(0)=x_{i} and by definition γ~1​(0)=x∞\widetilde{\gamma}_{1}(0)=x_{\infty}. We can now choose a small neighborhood B2B_{2} of r2r_{2} as above (that is, so that any flow line asymptotic to r2r_{2} is transverse to ∂B2\partial B_{2}, and other flow lines that intersect B2B_{2} either intersect ∂B2\partial B_{2} transversely twice or are tangent to ∂B2\partial B_{2}). We now know there is some t1>0t_{1}>0 such that γ~1​(t1)\widetilde{\gamma}_{1}(t_{1}) is the unique intersection point of γ~1\widetilde{\gamma}_{1} with ∂B2\partial B_{2}. Since the gradient flow is smooth and the initial conditions of the γi\gamma_{i} converge to that of γ~1\widetilde{\gamma}_{1}, we know that there is some N1N_{1} such that for i≥N1i\geq N_{1}, γi\gamma_{i} also intersects ∂B2\partial B_{2} transversely. Since the γi\gamma_{i} do not limit to r2r_{2} we know they must intersect ∂B2\partial B_{2} at another point. Denote this point by xi2x_{i}^{2}. The sequence {xi2}\{x^{2}_{i}\} converges to a point x∞2x^{2}_{\infty}. We now let γ~2\widetilde{\gamma}_{2} be the flow line through x∞2x^{2}_{\infty}.

We can continue with this process until we find a γ~n\widetilde{\gamma}_{n} that flows to qq.

Exercise 2.47.

Prove this last statement. Specifically, why must one of the trajectories in the broken flow line eventually end at qq?

To see that the sequence γi\gamma_{i} converges to the broken flow line ∪i=1nγ~i\cup_{i=1}^{n}\widetilde{\gamma}_{i} we note that one may take the balls BiB_{i} used to find the γ~i\widetilde{\gamma}_{i} to be arbitrarily small, so points of the γi\gamma_{i} in these balls are very close to the images of the γ~i\widetilde{\gamma}_{i}. There are finite intervals of time for which the γi\gamma_{i} and γ~i\widetilde{\gamma}_{i} are outside the BiB_{i}, and thus by the smoothness of the flow, we know for large enough jj, the images of the γj\gamma_{j} will be very close to the images of the γ~i\widetilde{\gamma}_{i}.

Exercise 2.48.

Carefully write out this convergence argument.

Remark 2.49.

We note that the geometry of gradient flows that determines the “compactness” result is simply the fact that flow lines of vector fields depend smoothly on their initial conditions.

2.5.4. Morse Theorem 44

We now turn to the “gluing” theorem. The basic idea here is to build an “approximate flow line” and then prove there is a unique actual flow line associated with it. The second step is accomplished by a theorem of Floer [20, Proposition 24] who generalized prior results of Picard and Newton. More specifically, Newton gave an iterative scheme to find zeros of a real-valued function, and if one has an “approximate zero” and one has appropriate control over the derivative of the function, then one can guarantee there is actually a zero near the approximate zero. Picard then gave an iterative scheme to find a solution to an initial value problem with similar control over where the solution is relative to an approximate solution. Floer finds a version of these ideas that can be applied to partial differential equations. We will not state Floer’s result here as it is a bit technical, but we give an indication of why one would hope to be able to “guess” an almost solution to an equation and then hope that there is an actual solution nearby. To this end, suppose we have a function f:ℝ→ℝf\colon\thinspace\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R} and want to find a zero of ff. Write ff as

f​(x)=f​(0)+L​(x)+N​(x)f(x)=f(0)+L(x)+N(x)

where LL is a linear map (given by multiplication by f′​(0)f^{\prime}(0)) and N​(x)N(x) is a non-linear map. Suppose we know that f​(x0)f(x_{0}) is very small relative to f′​(0)f^{\prime}(0) (that is, the norm of LL), then if NN were not there, we know there would be a zero of ff very close to x0x_{0}. Now, if N​(x)N(x) is very small in comparison to L​(x)L(x) for all xx near x0x_{0}, then one can also conclude that there is a zero of ff near x0x_{0}, and we can estimate how close the zero is to x0x_{0} in terms of a bound on L−1​N​(x)L^{-1}N(x).

Exercise 2.50.

Work out the details of the above discussion.

The above gives a very simple indication of the type of information and bounds Floer uses to prove his result.

We are now left to see how to construct an approximate flow line to a give broken flow line. Recall that we saw above that the space of parameterized flow lines ℳ¯qp\underline{\mathcal{M}}^{p}_{q} is the preimage of the zero section of

ϕ:(𝒫qp×P)→E:(γ,g)↦γ′​(t)+V​(γ​(t)).\phi\colon\thinspace(\mathcal{P}^{p}_{q}\times P)\to E\colon\thinspace(\gamma,g)\mapsto\gamma^{\prime}(t)+V(\gamma(t)).

So we want to find an approximate zero of ϕ\phi and then use a theorem of Floer to prove that there is an actual zero of ϕ\phi nearby and actually parameterize the nearby zeros.

Suppose γ~1∈ℳrp\widetilde{\gamma}_{1}\in\mathcal{M}^{p}_{r} and γ~2∈ℳqr\widetilde{\gamma}_{2}\in\mathcal{M}^{r}_{q} form a broken flow line. We can define a new path from pp to qq as follows. For a given positive number ρ\rho choose a parameterization of the flow lines so that γ~1​(−1)\widetilde{\gamma}_{1}(-1) and γ~2​(1)\widetilde{\gamma}_{2}(1) are the points where the flow lines intersect the ball B2B_{2} of radius ρ\rho about rr discussed in the previous section. Now the path γ~1​#ρ​γ~2\widetilde{\gamma}_{1}\#_{\rho}\widetilde{\gamma}_{2} will be γ~1\widetilde{\gamma}_{1} for t≤−1t\leq-1, γ~2\widetilde{\gamma}_{2} for t≥1t\geq 1 and for t∈[−1,1]t\in[-1,1] will be a (well-chosen) path in B2B_{2}. See Figure 12.

One can see that γ~1​#ρ​γ~2\widetilde{\gamma}_{1}\#_{\rho}\widetilde{\gamma}_{2} is “almost” a geodesic, in the sence that for most tt values it is a geodesic, and if one chooses the path correctly for t∈[−1,1]t\in[-1,1] then it is not far from being a geodesic on all of ℝ\mathbb{R} (notice that this path can be chosen to have very small derivitive and the grandient is also very small here so their difference is very small). With some work, one may use an infinite-dimensional version of the above mentioned result to show that near γ~1​#ρ​γ~2\widetilde{\gamma}_{1}\#_{\rho}\widetilde{\gamma}_{2} there is a 11-parameter family of flow lines in ℳqp\mathcal{M}^{p}_{q} that converge to the broken flow line γ~1∪γ~2\widetilde{\gamma}_{1}\cup\widetilde{\gamma}_{2}.

2.5.5. Another approach

Given a critical point pp of a Morse function f:M→ℝf\colon\thinspace M\to\mathbb{R} and a metric gg on MM, we can define the unstable manifold of pp to be

Up={x∈M​ such that ​limt→−∞γx​(t)=p},U_{p}=\{x\in M\text{ such that }\lim_{t\to-\infty}\gamma_{x}(t)=p\},

and the stable manifold of pp to be

Sp={x∈M​ such that ​limt→∞γx​(t)=p}.S_{p}=\{x\in M\text{ such that }\lim_{t\to\infty}\gamma_{x}(t)=p\}.

These are also sometimes referred to as the descending manifold and ascending manifold, respectively. One may prove, [3], that UpU_{p} and SpS_{p} are both open disks of dimension kk and n−kn-k, respectively, where k=index⁡pk=\operatorname{index}p.

We note that

ℳqp=Up∩Sq.\mathcal{M}^{p}_{q}=U_{p}\cap S_{q}.

One must interpret this equation correctly. Specifically, Up∩SqU_{p}\cap S_{q} is the image of the elements in ℳqp\mathcal{M}^{p}_{q}. So we can define Morse homology by counting the flow lines in Up∩SqU_{p}\cap S_{q}. One can interpret Morse Theorem 11 as the statement that all the stable and unstable manifolds of the critical points of ff are transverse to each other. Some approaches to Morse homology do not define the moduli spaces ℳqp\mathcal{M}^{p}_{q}, but instead focus on UpU_{p} and SpS_{p}. While this is a fine approach, the one presented above gives a better introduction to other homology theories. The main reason we mention this approach is the following fact.

Exercise 2.51.

Show that one may give MM the structure of a C​WCW complex using the unstable manifolds.

With this observation, one can easily identify Morse homology with cellular homology (which is, of course, known to be isomorphic to singular homology).

3. Lagrangian Floer homology

One can think of Lagrangian Floer homology as an infinite-dimensional version of Morse homology applied to the “action functional on the space of paths between two Lagrangian submanifolds”, but it is not possible to carry out the details. The reason for this is that the “gradient flow equation” for the action functional turns out to be an elliptic partial differential equation (as opposed to a parabolic equation which is the infinite dimensional analog of the finite dimensional gradient flow equation discussed above). Elliptic equations do not typically have solutions for “initial value problems”. More specifically, given a path between the two Lagrangian submanifolds, one cannot expect to find a solution to these equations, and thus the definitions of the moduli spaces above do not work in this case. Luckily, we can reformulate the above moduli spaces in terms of this elliptic equation. More specifically, Floer reformulated this idea in terms of pseudo-holomorphic curves. We will forgo this intuitive idea behind the theory and go straight to the definition of Lagrangian Floer theory that can be made rigorous.

We begin by recalling a few basic facts from symplectic geometry to set the stage for Lagrangian Floer theory. The objects counted in the boundary map defining Lagrangian Floer theory are pseudo-holomorphic curves, so we discuss these in the next section. As we saw above, compactness issues are key to defining a homology theory, so in Section 3.3, we discuss Gromov compactness, which details the behavior of limits of pseudo-holomorphic curves (under nice conditions). With this background in hand, we then turn to defining Lagrangian Floer homology in Section 3.4, and then end with a section discussing applications of Lagrangian Floer homology.

3.1. A little symplectic geometry

There are many good introductions to symplectic geometry, for example [5, 36]. We refer the reader to those sources for a more comprehensive introduction to the subject, but discuss a few basic definitions and results here to set the stage for Lagrangian Floer homology.

A symplectic manifold is a pair (X,ω)(X,\omega), where XX is a manifold and ω\omega is a 22-form that is closed, that is d​ω=0d\omega=0, and non-degenerate, that is if at any point x∈Xx\in X have have that ω​(v,w)=0\omega(v,w)=0 for all w∈Tx​Xw\in T_{x}X implies that v=0v=0. We call ω\omega a symplectic form, or symplectic structure, on XX. Some of the first things one observes about a symplectic manifold is that it dimension must be even, say 2​n2n dimensional, and ω\omega being non-degenerate is equivalently to ωn\omega^{n} being a volume form on XX (that is that ωn\omega^{n} is a non-zero 2​n2n-form at all points of XX), where ωn\omega^{n} means the nn-fold wedge product of ω\omega with itself.

Example 3.1.

The most basic example of a symplectic manifold is (ℝ2​n,ωs​t​d)(\mathbb{R}^{2n},\omega_{std}), where

ωs​t​d=∑i=1nd​xi∧d​yi,\omega_{std}=\sum_{i=1}^{n}dx_{i}\wedge dy_{i},

and (x1,y1,…,xn,yn)(x_{1},y_{1},\ldots,x_{n},y_{n}) are Euclidean coordinates on ℝ2​n\mathbb{R}^{2n}. One may easily check that ωs​t​d=−d​λs​t​d\omega_{std}=-d\lambda_{std} where

λs​t​d=∑i=1nyi​d​xi,\lambda_{std}=\sum_{i=1}^{n}y_{i}\,dx_{i},

and so ωs​t​d\omega_{std} is clearly closed. It is also a simple exercise to see that ωn\omega^{n} is a volume form. So we see that (ℝ2​n,ωs​t​d)(\mathbb{R}^{2n},\omega_{std}) is indeed a sympletic manifold.

In general, if a symplectic form ω\omega is the differential of a 11-form, then we call it an exact symplectic form. So the above example is an example of an exact symplectic form.

Example 3.2.

Another key example of a symplectic manifold is (T∗​M,ωc​a​n)(T^{*}M,\omega_{can}) where T∗​MT^{*}M is the cotangent bundle of any smooth manifold MM and ωc​a​n\omega_{can} is defined as follows. In local coordinates (q1,…,qn)(q_{1},\ldots,q_{n}) on an open set UU in MM we can take local coordinates for T∗​U=U×ℝnT^{*}U=U\times\mathbb{R}^{n} to be (q1,…,qn,p1,…,pn)(q_{1},\ldots,q_{n},p_{1},\ldots,p_{n}) where the last nn-coordinates are coordinates for ℝn\mathbb{R}^{n} in the basis {d​q1,…,d​qn)\{dq_{1},\ldots,dq_{n}). We can now define

λc​a​n=∑i=1npi​d​qi\lambda_{can}=\sum_{i=1}^{n}p_{i}\,dq_{i}

on UU.

Exercise 3.3.

Show that this defines a 11-form on all of T∗​MT^{*}M. Specifically, cover MM in coordinate charts and show that the λc​a​n\lambda_{can} defined above in each of these coordinate charts defines the same 11-form whenever the coordinate charts overlap.

Thus we have λc​a​n\lambda_{can} defined on all of T∗​MT^{*}M so we define ωc​a​n=−d​λc​a​n\omega_{can}=-d\lambda_{can}. This gives a closed 22-form that is easy to verify is also non-degenerate.

Symplectic geometry originally grew out of an elegant reformulation of classical mechanics and has many beautiful and deep applications. For the sake of brevity, we refer the reader to the references mentioned at the beginning of this section for more details.

A fundamental theorem in symplectic geometry, called the Darboux theorem, says that for any symplectic manifold (X,ω)(X,\omega) and any point x∈Xx\in X there is a neighborhood UU of xx in XX, a neighborhood VV of the origin in ℝ2​n\mathbb{R}^{2n}, and a diffeomorphisms ϕ:U→V\phi\colon\thinspace U\to V such that ϕ∗​ωs​t​d=ω\phi^{*}\omega_{std}=\omega. That is, any symplectic manifold, locally, looks like the example above! We note that a diffeomorphism ϕ\phi from one symplectic manifold, (X,ω)(X,\omega), to another, (X′,ω′)(X^{\prime},\omega^{\prime}), is called a symplectomorphism.

A common means to build symplectomorphisms is via Hamiltonian flows. Specifically, given a function H:X→ℝH\colon\thinspace X\to\mathbb{R} on a symplectic manifold (X,ω)(X,\omega) there is a unique vector field vHv_{H} on XX such that

ιvH​ω=d​H.\iota_{v_{H}}\omega=dH.
Exercise 3.4.

Using the non-degeneracy of ω\omega, show that such a vhv_{h} exists and is unique.

The function HH is called a Hamiltonian and the vector field vHv_{H} is called a Hamiltonian vector field. Let ϕH:M→M\phi_{H}\colon\thinspace M\to M be the time 11 flow of vHv_{H}. One may easily check that ϕH\phi_{H} is a symplectomorphism. We call ϕH\phi_{H} a Hamiltonian symplectomorphism. We note that it is not important that we choose the time 11 flow of vHv_{H} because by scaling HH we scale vHv_{H} and the time 11 flow of these rescalled vector fields is simply the time tt flow of vHv_{H} for some t≠1t\not=1. We note that on most symplectic manifolds, there are symplectomorphisms that are not Hamiltonian symplectomorphisms, but we do not pursue this matter here.

The last ingredient we need for our discussion of Lagrangian Floer homology is Lagrangian submanifolds. A Lagrangian submanifold LL of a symplectic manifold (X,ω)(X,\omega) is a submanifold of XX having half the dimension of XX and for which ω\omega vanishes on the tangent space of LL.

Example 3.5.

Any oriented surface Σ\Sigma can be made a symplectic manifold simply by choosing an area form ω\omega on Σ\Sigma. We note that any 11-dimensional manifold in Σ\Sigma will be Lagrangian. So we have lots of examples of Lagrangian submanifolds.

Exercise 3.6.

Show that the product of symplectic manifolds is symplectic in a natural way, and the product of Lagrangian submanifolds in each symplectic manifold is a Lagrangian submanifold of the product symplectic manifold.

From the previous exercise, we can find many Lagrangian tori in symplectic manifolds built as products of orientable surfaces.

We end this section with an example showing the key nature of Lagrangian submanifolds in the study of symplectic manifolds.

Example 3.7.

Let (X,ω)(X,\omega) be a symplectic manifold and ϕ:X→X\phi\colon\thinspace X\to X be a symplectomorphism. We now consider the symplectic manifold (W,ωW)(W,\omega_{W}) where W=X×XW=X\times X and ωW=ω⊕−ω\omega_{W}=\omega\oplus-\omega. To be a bit more precise about the definition of ωW\omega_{W} we let πi:W→X\pi_{i}\colon\thinspace W\to X be the projection of WW to the it​hi^{th} factor of WW and then the more proper definition of ωW\omega_{W} is

ωW=π1∗​ω−π2∗​ω.\omega_{W}=\pi_{1}^{*}\omega-\pi_{2}^{*}\omega.

Now, if we let Γϕ\Gamma_{\phi} be the graph of ϕ\phi, that is Γϕ={(x,ϕ​(x)):x∈X}\Gamma_{\phi}=\{(x,\phi(x)):x\in X\}, then Γϕ\Gamma_{\phi} is a Lagrangian submanifold of (W,ωW)(W,\omega_{W}). Indeed, consider

Φ:X→W:x↦(x,ϕ​(x)).\Phi\colon\thinspace X\to W\colon\thinspace x\mapsto(x,\phi(x)).

This map clearly parameterizes Γϕ\Gamma_{\phi} and the restriction of ωW\omega_{W} to the tangent space of Γϕ\Gamma_{\phi} is simply Φ∗​ωW\Phi^{*}\omega_{W}. We now compute
