Φ∗​ωW=Φ∗​π1∗​ω−Φ∗​π2∗​ω=ω−ϕ∗​ω=0,\Phi^{*}\omega_{W}=\Phi^{*}\pi_{1}^{*}\omega-\Phi^{*}\pi_{2}^{*}\omega=\omega-\phi^{*}\omega=0,

since ϕ\phi is a symplectomorphism.

Exercise 3.8.

Considering the previous example, show that symplectomorphisms of (X,ω)(X,\omega) correspond to a certain subset of Lagrangian submanifolds of (W,ωW)(W,\omega_{W}).

3.2. Pseudo-holomorphic curves

Pseudo-holomorphic curves have been a cornerstone of the study of symplectic manifolds since their introduction by Gromov in [28], and, in particular, are used in the definition of Lagrangian Floer homology. In this section, we will discuss a few basic facts about pseudo-holomorphic curves.

An almost-complex structure on a manifold MM is a bundle map

J:T​M→T​MJ\colon\thinspace TM\to TM

covering the identity on MM such that J2=−idT​MJ^{2}=-\text{id}_{TM}. Notice that JJ gives each tangent space Tx​MT_{x}M the structure of a complex vector space.

Example 3.9.

Show that a complex manifold has a canonical almost-complex structure.

We note that not all almost-complex structures on a manifold come from a complex structure on the manifold. In particular, there are many manifolds that do not admit a complex structure, but do admit almost-complex structures. However, it is true that on an oriented surface Σ\Sigma complex structures and almost-complex structures are equivalent.

Now, given a closed Riemannian surface Σ\Sigma then it has a natural complex structure (namely, one simply rotates a tangent vector anti-clockwise by π/2\pi/2). We will denote this almost-complex structure jj. Now given a manifold MM with an almost-complex structure JJ, we say a map

u:Σ→Mu\colon\thinspace\Sigma\to M

is pseudo-holomorphic if it intertwines the almost-complex structures, that is

d​u∘j=J∘d​udu\circ j=J\circ du

where d​u:T​Σ→T​Mdu\colon\thinspace T\Sigma\to TM is the derivative of uu.

Example 3.10.

If M=ℂM=\mathbb{C} with the almost-complex structure coming from the complex structure on ℂ\mathbb{C}, then show that a function u:Σ→ℂu\colon\thinspace\Sigma\to\mathbb{C} is pseudo-holomorphic if and only if it is holomorphic in the sense of complex analysis.

Below, we will need to have our complex structure related to a symplectic structure. To discuss this relation, we consider the symplectic manifold (X,ω)(X,\omega). An almost-complex structure on XX is said to be tamed by ω\omega if

ω​(v,J​v)>0,\omega(v,Jv)>0,

for any non-zero tangent vector vv. We also say that JJ is compatible with ω\omega if JJ is tamed by ω\omega and ω\omega is JJ-invariant, which means that ω​(J​v,J​w)=ω​(v,w)\omega(Jv,Jw)=\omega(v,w). The main result we will need about compatible almost-complex structure is the following.

Lemma 3.11.

The space of almost-complex structures compatible with a fixed symplectic structure is non-empty and convex.

The proof of this lemma is straightforward, but a bit involved. It can be found in the references mentioned at the beginning of this section.

3.3. Gromov compactness

As we saw with Morse homology, understanding the compactness of the geometric objects counted in the homology boundary map is a key to making a homology theory. So, before we start to define Lagrangian Floer homology, we will discuss what can happen when considering sequences of pseudo-holomorphic curves.

To this end, we first define “singular” Riemannian surfaces. We start with a surface Σ\Sigma and a collection of properly embedded curves γ1,…,γn\gamma_{1},\ldots,\gamma_{n}. See Figure 13.

Let Σ^\widehat{\Sigma} be the result of collapsing each γi\gamma_{i} to a point, which we denote by ziz_{i}. See Figure 14. We note that if jj is an almost-complex structure on Σ\Sigma, then it induces one on Σ^−{z1,⋯,zn}\widehat{\Sigma}-\{z_{1},\cdots,z_{n}\}, which we still denote by jj. We call (Σ^,j)(\widehat{\Sigma},j) a cusped curve.

We note there is a natrual quotient map π:Σ→Σ^\pi\colon\thinspace\Sigma\to\widehat{\Sigma} and we define the singular set of Σ^\widehat{\Sigma} to be s​(Σ^)={z1,…,zn}s(\widehat{\Sigma})=\{z_{1},\ldots,z_{n}\}.

Now, given an oriented Riemannian surface (Σ,j)(\Sigma,j) we get a metric on Σ\Sigma by setting h​(v,w)=Ω​(v,j​w)h(v,w)=\Omega(v,jw) where Ω\Omega is any araa form on Σ\Sigma giving the orientation on Σ\Sigma. Given an almost-complex structure JJ compatible with a symplectic form ω\omega on a manifold XX can now define the action of a map u:Σ→Xu\colon\thinspace\Sigma\to X to be

𝒜​(u)=∫Σ‖d​u‖2​Ω,\mathcal{A}(u)=\int_{\Sigma}\|du\|^{2}\Omega,

where ‖d​u‖\|du\| at the point xx is defined to be

∥du∥x=max{∥du(v)∥g:v∈TxΣ,∥v∥h=1}\|du\|_{x}=\text{max}\{\|du(v)\|_{g}:v\in T_{x}\Sigma,\|v\|_{h}=1\}

and ‖v‖h\|v\|_{h} is the length of vv measured with respect to the metric hh and similarly for ‖w‖g\|w\|_{g} where g​(v,w)=ω​(v,J​w)g(v,w)=\omega(v,Jw). So pointwise ‖d​u‖x\|du\|_{x} measures the maximal distortion of vectors in Tx​ΣT_{x}\Sigma by d​uxdu_{x} and 𝒜​(u)\mathcal{A}(u) is just the integral of this over Σ\Sigma. We note that one can similarly define the action for a map from a cusped curve to XX.

Exercise 3.12.

Show that

𝒜​(u)≤∫Σu∗​ω,\mathcal{A}(u)\leq\int_{\Sigma}u^{*}\omega,

and that one has equality if and only if uu is pseudo-holomorphic.

Given a sequence of pseudo-holomorphic curves uk:(Σ,jk)→(X,J)u_{k}\colon\thinspace(\Sigma,j_{k})\to(X,J) and a cusped curve π:Σ→Σ^\pi:\Sigma\to\widehat{\Sigma} with a almost-complex structure jj, then we say the sequence {uk}\{u_{k}\} converges to u∞:Σ^→Xu_{\infty}\colon\thinspace\widehat{\Sigma}\to X if

  1. (1)

    there are maps ϕk:Σ→Σ^\phi_{k}\colon\thinspace\Sigma\to\widehat{\Sigma} and curves γ1k,…,γnk\gamma_{1}^{k},\ldots,\gamma_{n}^{k} such that

    ϕk|Σ−⁣∪γi:(Σ−∪γi)→(Σ^−s(Σ^))\phi_{k}|_{\Sigma-\cup\gamma_{i}}\colon\thinspace(\Sigma-\cup\gamma_{i})\to(\widehat{\Sigma}-s(\widehat{\Sigma}))

    is a diffeomorphism,

  2. (2)

    the sequence of almost-complex structures {(ϕk|Σ−⁣∪γi∗jk}\{(\phi_{k}|_{\Sigma-\cup\gamma_{i}}^{*}j_{k}\} C∞C^{\infty}-converges to jj on compact subsets as k→∞k\to\infty,

  3. (3)

    the sequence {uk∘ϕk−1|Σ^−s​(Σ^)}\{u_{k}\circ\phi_{k}^{-1}|_{\widehat{\Sigma}-s(\widehat{\Sigma})}\} C∞C^{\infty}-converges to u∞|Σ^−s​(Σ^)u_{\infty}|_{\widehat{\Sigma}-s(\widehat{\Sigma})} on compact subsets as k→∞k\to\infty, and

  4. (4)

    limk→∞𝒜​(uk)=𝒜​(u∞)\lim_{k\to\infty}\mathcal{A}(u_{k})=\mathcal{A}(u_{\infty}).

Theorem 3.13 (Gromov compactness, [28]).

Let (X,ω)(X,\omega) be a symplectic manifold with an almost-complex structure JJ. If uk:(Σ,jk)→(X,J)u_{k}\colon\thinspace(\Sigma,j_{k})\to(X,J) is a sequence of pseudo-holomorphic maps with bounded action (here if, ∂Σ≠∅\partial\Sigma\not=\emptyset then uk​(∂Σ)⊂Lu_{k}(\partial\Sigma)\subset L for some fixed Lagrangian submanifold LL of XX), then some subsequence of {uk}\{u_{k}\} converges to a cusp curve u∞:(Σ^,j)→(X,J)u_{\infty}:(\widehat{\Sigma},j)\to(X,J). (We note that s​(Σ^)s(\widehat{\Sigma}) could be empty, in which case the uku_{k} converge to a pseudo-holomorphic curve.)

Remark 3.14.

We note that Gromov’s original theorem did not require a symplectic form, but merely a Riemannian metric, so that the action of the pseudo-holomorphic curves can be measured. We state this version as it is all that we will need below.

To get some sense of this type of convergence, we look at an example.

Example 3.15.

Let Σ=S2\Sigma=S^{2} be the 11-point compactification of ℂ\mathbb{C} (with any fixed area form and almost-complex structure) and consider the maps

uk:S2→S2×S2:z↦(z,1/k​z).u_{k}\colon\thinspace S^{2}\to S^{2}\times S^{2}\colon\thinspace z\mapsto(z,1/kz).

We can see the graphs of these functions in Figure 15.

We notice that as k→∞k\to\infty these maps converge to u∞1:S2→S2×S2:z↦(z,0)u_{\infty}^{1}\colon\thinspace S^{2}\to S^{2}\times S^{2}:z\mapsto(z,0) way from z=0z=0. The problem is that the gradient of the uku_{k} is blowing up at z=0z=0. So to see what the uku_{k} converge to in a neighborhood of 0 we need to reparameterize the uku_{k}. This is similar to when we studied geodesics, and we were only interested in the image of the curves and not their parameterization. If we reparameterize the uku_{k} by the holomorphic map ϕ:S2→S2:z↦1/k​z\phi\colon\thinspace S^{2}\to S^{2}:z\mapsto 1/kz we see

uk∘ϕ:S2→S2×S2:z↦(1/k​z,z).u_{k}\circ\phi\colon\thinspace S^{2}\to S^{2}\times S^{2}\colon\thinspace z\mapsto(1/kz,z).

And these maps converge to u∞2:S2→S2×S2:z↦(0,z)u_{\infty}^{2}\colon\thinspace S^{2}\to S^{2}\times S^{2}\colon\thinspace z\mapsto(0,z). So the sequence of pseudo-holomorphic curves uk​(S2)u_{k}(S^{2}) converges to the cusped curve u∞:S2^→S2×S2u_{\infty}:\widehat{S^{2}}\to S^{2}\times S^{2} where S2^\widehat{S^{2}} is a wedge of two S2S^{2}s and u∞u_{\infty} is u∞1u_{\infty}^{1} on one of the spheres and u∞2u_{\infty}^{2} on the other.

We note in this simple example that we were able to find one global reparameterization to “see” both the limiting spheres, but in general, one might need to reparameterize each of the uku_{k} separately, and this is the role of the ϕk\phi_{k} in the definition of convergence.

3.4. Defining Lagrangian Floer homology

We are now ready to define Lagrangian Floer homology. We start with a symplectic manifold (X,ω)(X,\omega) and two Lagrangian submanifolds L0L_{0} and L1L_{1} of XX. Lagrangian Floer homology will be an invariant of the pair L0L_{0} and L1L_{1} up to Hamiltonian isotopy of either of the Lagrangian submanifolds.

We first note that one may slightly isotop the LiL_{i} so that they are transverse to each other. The chain complex for Lagrangian Floer homology is

F​C​(L0,L1)= the free R-module generated by ​L0∩L1,FC(L_{0},L_{1})=\text{ the free $R$-module generated by }L_{0}\cap L_{1},

where RR is some ring of field like ℤ\mathbb{Z} or ℤ/2​ℤ\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}.

To define the boundary map, we will consider “pseudo-holomorphic strips”. That is we will consider the surface ℝ×[0,1]\mathbb{R}\times[0,1] with coordinate (s,t)(s,t) and the complex structure that sends ∂∂t\frac{\partial}{\partial t} to ∂∂s\frac{\partial}{\partial s} and ∂∂s\frac{\partial}{\partial s} to −∂∂t-\frac{\partial}{\partial t}. Thus, if we choose an almost-complex structure JJ on XX that is compatible with ω\omega (this is like choosing a Riemannian metric when studying Morse homology), then a map u:(ℝ×[0,1])→Xu\colon\thinspace(\mathbb{R}\times[0,1])\to X is pseudo-holomorphic if and only if

∂u∂s+J​∂u∂t=0\frac{\partial u}{\partial s}+J\frac{\partial u}{\partial t}=0

where of course ∂u∂s\frac{\partial u}{\partial s} is the image of the vector ∂∂s\frac{\partial}{\partial s} under d​udu and similarly for ∂u∂t\frac{\partial u}{\partial t}.

Exercise 3.16.

Prove the statement characterizing when uu is pseudo-holomorphic.

We can also consider a family of almost complex structures JtJ_{t} compatible with ω\omega and consider pseudo-holomorphic strips u:(ℝ×[0,1])→Xu\colon\thinspace(\mathbb{R}\times[0,1])\to X that satisfy

∂u∂s+Jt​∂u∂t=0.\frac{\partial u}{\partial s}+J_{t}\frac{\partial u}{\partial t}=0.

This extra flexibility in the definition of a pseudo-holomorphic strip can be helpful in proving various technical results.

Now given two point x,y∈L0∩L1x,y\in L_{0}\cap L_{1} we consider the space

ℳyx={u:(ℝ×[0,1])\displaystyle\mathcal{M}^{x}_{y}=\{u\colon\thinspace(\mathbb{R}\times[0,1]) →X​ such that\displaystyle\to X\text{ such that}
1) u​(s,i)∈Li for i=0,1,\displaystyle\text{1) $u(s,i)\in L_{i}$ for $i=0,1$},
2) lims→−∞u​(s,t)=x,\displaystyle\text{2) $\lim_{s\to-\infty}u(s,t)=x$},
3) lims→∞u​(s,t)=y, and\displaystyle\text{3) $\lim_{s\to\infty}u(s,t)=y$},\text{ and}
4) u is pseudo-holomorphic}/ℝ.\displaystyle\text{4) $u$ is pseudo-holomorphic}\}/\mathbb{R}.

To understand the ℝ\mathbb{R} action above we note that Ts0:(ℝ×[0,1])→(ℝ×[0,1]):(s,t)↦(s+s0,t)T_{s_{0}}\colon\thinspace(\mathbb{R}\times[0,1])\to(\mathbb{R}\times[0,1])\colon\thinspace(s,t)\mapsto(s+s_{0},t) is a holomorphic map so given any pseudo-holomorphic u:(ℝ×[0,1])→Xu\colon\thinspace(\mathbb{R}\times[0,1])\to X the map u∘Ts0u\circ T_{s_{0}} is also pseudo-holomorphic and hence ℝ\mathbb{R} acts on the set of such psedudo-holomorphic maps.

It will be important for us to keep track of the homotopy class of an element u∈ℳyxu\in\mathcal{M}^{x}_{y}. To this end we define the set

π2(x,y)={homotopy classes of maps\displaystyle\pi_{2}(x,y)=\{\text{homotopy classes of maps } u:(ℝ×[0,1])→X​ such that\displaystyle u\colon\thinspace(\mathbb{R}\times[0,1])\to X\text{ such that}
1) u​(s,i)∈Li for i=0,1,\displaystyle\text{1) $u(s,i)\in L_{i}$ for $i=0,1$},
2) lims→−∞u​(s,t)=x,and\displaystyle\text{2) $\lim_{s\to-\infty}u(s,t)=x$},\text{and}
3) lims→∞u​(s,t)=y}\displaystyle\text{3) $\lim_{s\to\infty}u(s,t)=y$}\}

For each h∈π2​(x,y)h\in\pi_{2}(x,y) we can now consider the spaces

ℳyx​(h)={u∈ℳyx​ such that ​u∈h}\mathcal{M}^{x}_{y}(h)=\{u\in\mathcal{M}^{x}_{y}\text{ such that }u\in h\}

and notice that

ℳyx=⋃h∈π2​(x,y)ℳyx​(h).\mathcal{M}^{x}_{y}=\bigcup_{h\in\pi_{2}(x,y)}\mathcal{M}^{x}_{y}(h).

Before we can state the main theorems to construct our boundary map, we need to consider an “index” for xx and yy. In Morse theory, we could assign an index to each critical point, and we could then determine the dimension of the moduli space of flowlines in terms of these indices. Unfortunately, we do not have a well-defined index for each x∈L0∩L1x\in L_{0}\cap L_{1}, but we do have a “relative index” for a pair of points x,y∈L0∩L1x,y\in L_{0}\cap L_{1}, and this will be sufficient to define the boundary map for our chain complex. This relative index is provided by the Maslov index or Maslov class.

3.4.1. The Maslov class

We begin by considering ℝ2​n=ℂn\mathbb{R}^{2n}=\mathbb{C}^{n} with its standard symplectic structure ωs​t​d\omega_{std} defined in Example 3.1. We can now let ℒ​(ℂn)\mathcal{L}(\mathbb{C}^{n}) be the space of linear Lagrangian subspaces in ℂn\mathbb{C}^{n}. It is not too hard to show

π1​(ℒ​(ℂn))≅ℤ\pi_{1}(\mathcal{L}(\mathbb{C}^{n}))\cong\mathbb{Z}

and there is a “preferred” generator, see [41].

Exercise 3.17.

Prove this for when n=1n=1.
Hint: Notice that ℒ​(ℂ)\mathcal{L}(\mathbb{C}) is simply ℝ​P2\mathbb{R}P^{2} and a natural generator for its fundamental group is the loop that starts with the xx-axis and rotates through an angle of π\pi.

Now consider two linear Lagrangian subspaces Λ0\Lambda_{0} and Λ1\Lambda_{1} in ℂn\mathbb{C}^{n} that intersect transversely.

Exercise 3.18.

Show that there is a complex linear map I:ℂn→ℂnI\colon\thinspace\mathbb{C}^{n}\to\mathbb{C}^{n} such that I​(Λ1)=Λ0I(\Lambda_{1})=\Lambda_{0}.

Using II from the exercise, we can define a map a path in ℒ​(ℂn)\mathcal{L}(\mathbb{C}^{n}) from Λ1\Lambda_{1} to Λ0\Lambda_{0} given by

γΛ0,Λ1​(t)=et​I​Λ1=((cos⁡t)​ idℂn+(sin⁡t)​I)​Λ1,\gamma_{\Lambda_{0},\Lambda_{1}}(t)=e^{tI}\Lambda_{1}=\left((\cos t)\text{ id}_{\mathbb{C}^{n}}+(\sin t)I\right)\Lambda_{1},

as tt goes from 0 to π/2\pi/2. See Figure 16.

Now suppose that u:ℝ×[0,1]→Xu\colon\thinspace\mathbb{R}\times[0,1]\to X is a representative of an element in π2​(x,y)\pi_{2}(x,y). Consider

u∗​T​X≅(ℝ×[0,1])×ℂn.u^{*}TX\cong(\mathbb{R}\times[0,1])\times\mathbb{C}^{n}.

Thus, if we have a linear Lagrangian subspace in Tx​XT_{x}X for some xx in the image of uu, then we can use the trivialization above to think of it as a Lagrangian subspace of ℂn\mathbb{C}^{n}, that is, as an element of ℒ​(ℂn)\mathcal{L}(\mathbb{C}^{n}). Thus restricting uu to ℝ×{i}\mathbb{R}\times\{i\}, for i=0,1i=0,1, will give paths γi​(t)=Tu​(t,i)​Li\gamma_{i}(t)=T_{u(t,i)}L_{i} in ℒ​(ℂn)\mathcal{L}(\mathbb{C}^{n}). We can think of extending the paths to [−∞,∞][-\infty,\infty] by continuity. So γi​(∞)\gamma_{i}(\infty) will be Ty​LiT_{y}L_{i} and γi​(−∞)\gamma_{i}(-\infty) will be Tx​LiT_{x}L_{i}. We can finally define the path

γu=γ0∗γγ1​(∞),γ0​(∞)∗γ1¯∗γγ0​(−∞),γ1​(−∞)¯\gamma_{u}=\gamma_{0}*\gamma_{\gamma_{1}(\infty),\gamma_{0}(\infty)}*\overline{\gamma_{1}}*\overline{\gamma_{\gamma_{0}(-\infty),\gamma_{1}(-\infty)}}

by concatenating the paths, where ∗* denotes concatenation and γ¯\overline{\gamma} is the path γ\gamma run backwards. See Figure 17.

It is clear that γu\gamma_{u} is a loop in ℒ​(ℂn)\mathcal{L}(\mathbb{C}^{n}) and so we define the Maslov index of uu to be the integer associated to the homotopy class of this loop

μ​(u)∈ℤ≅π1​(ℒ​(ℂn))\mu(u)\in\mathbb{Z}\cong\pi_{1}(\mathcal{L}(\mathbb{C}^{n}))

We are now ready to state the first main theorem.

One can also orient these moduli spaces. We note in the satement we need the Lagrangian submanifolds to be spin. Spin is a mild condition on a manifold that is not relevant for our main discussion, so we do not discuss it further.

3.4.2. Compactness and gluing

We have discussed compactness for pseudo-holomorphic curves in almost complex manifolds in Section 3.3, but we now consider the specific pseudo-holomorphic curves we will need in the definition of Floer homology.

Finally, we have a gluing theorem. We state a vague version of the theorem as the full theorem can be a bit technical.

We are now ready to return to the definition of Floer homology. Recall from the beginning of this section that given two Lagrangian submanifolds L0L_{0} and L1L_{1} of a symplectic manifold (X,ω)(X,\omega) (after isotoping them so they are transverse to each other), we defined the chain complex for Lagrangian Floer homology to be

F​C​(L0,L1)= the free R module generated by ​L0∩L1.FC(L_{0},L_{1})=\text{ the free $R$ module generated by }L_{0}\cap L_{1}.

We now define the chain map

∂:F​C​(L0,L1)→F​C​(L0,L1)\partial\colon\thinspace FC(L_{0},L_{1})\to FC(L_{0},L_{1})

on an element p+∈L0∩L1p_{+}\in L_{0}\cap L_{1} by

∂p+=∑p−∈L0∩L1(∑h∈π2​(p+,p−),μ​(h)=1|ℳp−p+​(h)/ℝ|​p−).\partial p_{+}=\sum_{p_{-}\in L_{0}\cap L_{1}}\left(\sum_{h\in\pi_{2}(p_{+},p_{-}),\mu(h)=1}|\mathcal{M}^{p_{+}}_{p_{-}}(h)/\mathbb{R}|\,p_{-}\right).

Recall that ℳp−p+​(h)\mathcal{M}^{p_{+}}_{p_{-}}(h) has an ℝ\mathbb{R} actions so ℳp−p+​(h)/ℝ\mathcal{M}^{p_{+}}_{p_{-}}(h)/\mathbb{R} is just its quotient by this action and |ℳp−p+​(h)/ℝ||\mathcal{M}^{p_{+}}_{p_{-}}(h)/\mathbb{R}| is the number of points in the 0-dimesnional manifold ℳp−p+​(h)/ℝ\mathcal{M}^{p_{+}}_{p_{-}}(h)/\mathbb{R}.

Remark 3.19.

For this sum to be finite, we need

(∗*) {𝒜​(ϕ):[ϕ]∈π2​(p+,p1)}<∞\{\mathcal{A}(\phi):[\phi]\in\pi_{2}(p_{+},p_{1})\}<\infty

This will happen if |π2​(p+,p−)||\pi_{2}(p_{+},p_{-})| is finite.

We now see when ∂\partial is a differential.

Lemma 3.20.

The map ∂\partial above is well-defined if (∗)(*) is holds. Moreover, if the limits (1) and (2) in Figure 18 do not occur, then ∂2=0\partial^{2}=0.

Proof.

To see that the map is well-defined, we need to see that |ℳp−p+​(h)/ℝ||\mathcal{M}^{p_{+}}_{p_{-}}(h)/\mathbb{R}| is compact (it is already a 0-manifold, so it will then be finite). Since we are assuming (∗)(*) we can use the Gromov Compactness Theorem, Theorem 3.13, to see that a sequence of pseudo-holomorphic strips in ℳp−p+​(h)\mathcal{M}^{p_{+}}_{p_{-}}(h) that does not converge to a pseudo-holomorphic strip must converge to a cusped strip as in Figure 18. But we are assuming that the limits (1) and (2) do not occur, so the only limit we can have is (3). That is, our sequence unu_{n} converges to a broken strip. That is it converges to the union of two strips, say u∞1u_{\infty}^{1} and u∞2u_{\infty}^{2}.

Exercise 3.21.

Show that

μ​(un)=μ​(u∞1)+μ​(u∞2).\mu(u_{n})=\mu(u_{\infty}^{1})+\mu(u_{\infty}^{2}).

Since we know that μ​(un)=1\mu(u_{n})=1, we see that one of the u∞iu_{\infty}^{i} must be in a 0-dimensional moduli space. But we know any pseudo-holomorphic strip is in a moduli space with an ℝ\mathbb{R}-action, so the space cannot have dimension 0. Thus, our sequence cannot limit to (3) and must limit to another pseudo-holomorphic strip. In particular, ℳp−p+​(h)\mathcal{M}^{p_{+}}_{p_{-}}(h) is compact.

The proof that ∂2=0\partial^{2}=0 is analogous to the proof given in Theorem 2.26 for ∂2=0\partial^{2}=0 in Morse homology. Specifically, see Figure 19.

There we see that if p−p_{-} is in ∂2p+\partial^{2}p_{+} (that is p−p_{-} is in ∂x\partial x where xx is in ∂p+\partial p_{+}), then the gluing theorem says there are nearby pseudo-holomorphic strips from p+p_{+} to p−p_{-} that converge to the strip from p+p_{+} to xx and from xx to p−p_{-}. Now, since limits (1) and (2) are not allowed, the only compactification of this moduli space is by another broken strip through some double point yy. ∎

Remark 3.22.

Notice that if the limit (2) in Figure 18 could occur, then the last diagram in Figure 19 could look like Figure 20.

In that case, p−p_{-} might only show up in ∂2p+\partial^{2}p_{+} an odd number of times, and hence ∂2p+≠0\partial^{2}p_{+}\not=0.

Given Lagrangian submanifolds L0L_{0} and L1L_{1} satisfying (∗)(*) and for which we do not have limits (1) and (2) in Figure 18, we can define the Floer homology of L0L_{0} and L1L_{1} to be

F​H​(L0,L1)=ker(∂:FC(L0,L1)→FC(L0,L1)))image⁡(∂:F​C​(L0,L1)→F​C​(L0,L1))FH(L_{0},L_{1})=\frac{\ker(\partial\colon\thinspace FC(L_{0},L_{1})\to FC(L_{0},L_{1})))}{\operatorname{image}(\partial\colon\thinspace FC(L_{0},L_{1})\to FC(L_{0},L_{1}))}
Remark 3.23.

We note that one can define a relative grading on F​H​(L0,L1)FH(L_{0},L_{1}) using the Maslov class, but this is a little involved (it might be only well-defined modulo some integer), so we do not address this here.

Recall, earlier we discussed that given a function H:X→ℝH\colon\thinspace X\to\mathbb{R}, there is a Hamiltonian vector field vHv_{H} defined by ιvH​ω=d​H\iota_{v_{H}}\omega=dH. Any time tt flow of vHv_{H} is called a Hamiltonian diffeomorphism.

Theorem 3.24.

Given a symplectic manifold (X,ω)(X,\omega) and Lagrangian submanifolds L0L_{0} and L1L_{1} such that the limits (1) and (2) in Floer Theorem 3 do not occur. If (∗)(*) holds, then F​H​(L0,L1)FH(L_{0},L_{1}) is independent of the almost complex structures JtJ_{t} chosen and

F​H​(ϕ​(L0),ψ​(L1))=F​H​(L0,L1)FH(\phi(L_{0}),\psi(L_{1}))=FH(L_{0},L_{1})

for any Hamiltonian diffeomorphisms ϕ\phi and ψ\psi.

The proof of this theorem is similar to the proof that Morse homology is independent of the chosen metric and Morse function.

3.5. Applications

In this section, we will consider a simple situation when Floer homology is well-defined and a surprising construction that allows one to define an invariant of 33-manifolds using Lagrangian Floer homology.

3.5.1. Exact Lagrangians submanifolds

Let (X,ω)(X,\omega) be an exact symplectic manifold, meaning that there is a 11-form λ\lambda such that d​λ=ωd\lambda=\omega.

Suppose that Σ\Sigma is a closed pseudo-holomorphic surface in XX. Then ω​(Tx​Σ)>0\omega(T_{x}\Sigma)>0 for all x∈Σx\in\Sigma (since, for any v∈Tx​Σv\in T_{x}\Sigma we know vv and J​vJv span Tx​ΣT_{x}\Sigma and ω​(v,J​v)>0\omega(v,Jv)>0 since JJ is tamed by ω\omega). Thus, we see that

𝒜​(Σ)=∫Σω>0,\mathcal{A}(\Sigma)=\int_{\Sigma}\omega>0,

but

∫Σω=∫Σ𝑑λ=∫∂Σλ=0.\int_{\Sigma}\omega=\int_{\Sigma}d\lambda=\int_{\partial\Sigma}\lambda=0.

This contradiction indicates that there are no closed pseudo-holomorphic surfaces in XX and thus limit (1) in Floer Theorem 3 cannot happen.

Now suppose that LL is an exact Lagrangian submanifold of XX. This means that there is some function f:L→ℝf\colon\thinspace L\to\mathbb{R} such that λ|L=d​f\lambda|_{L}=df. Given a holomorphic sufacse Σ\Sigma with boundary on LL, we note as above that

∫Σω>0,\int_{\Sigma}\omega>0,

but

∫Σω=∫∂Σλ=∫∂Σ𝑑f=∫∂(∂Σ)f=0.\int_{\Sigma}\omega=\int_{\partial\Sigma}\lambda=\int_{\partial\Sigma}df=\int_{\partial(\partial\Sigma)}f=0.

This contradiction shows that limit (2) in Floer Theorem 3 cannot happen.

Finally, if L0L_{0} and L1L_{1} are two exact Lagrangian submanifolds of XX, with corresponding functions f0f_{0} and f1f_{1}, and u:(ℝ×[0,1])→Xu\colon\thinspace(\mathbb{R}\times[0,1])\to X is a pseudo-holomorphic strip with boundary on L0L_{0} and L1L_{1} from the intersection points p+p_{+} to p−p_{-}, then

𝒜​(u)\displaystyle\mathcal{A}(u) =∫ℝ×[0,1]u∗​ω=∫∂(ℝ×[0,1])u∗​λ=∫−∞∞u∗​f1−∫−∞∞u∗​f0\displaystyle=\int_{\mathbb{R}\times[0,1]}u^{*}\omega=\int_{\partial(\mathbb{R}\times[0,1])}u^{*}\lambda=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}u^{*}f_{1}-\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}u^{*}f_{0}
=f1​(p+)−f1​(p−)−f0​(p+)+f0​(p−)\displaystyle=f_{1}(p_{+})-f_{1}(p_{-})-f_{0}(p_{+})+f_{0}(p_{-})

Thus, 𝒜\mathcal{A} is bounded on π2​(p+,p−)\pi_{2}(p_{+},p_{-}) and so (∗)(*) is true.

From the above, we see the following result holds.

Theorem 3.25.

Floer homology is well-defined for exact Lagrangian submanifolds in an exact symplectic manifold.

Now consider a Morse function f:M→ℝf\colon\thinspace M\to\mathbb{R} on a manifold MM. Let π:T∗​M→M\pi\colon\thinspace T^{*}M\to M be the projection map from the cotangent bundle of MM to MM. If we set F=f∘π:T∗​M→MF=f\circ\pi\colon\thinspace T^{*}M\to M we can let vFv_{F} be the Hamiltonian vector field associated to vFv_{F} on T∗​MT^{*}M using the canonical symplectic structure on T∗​MT^{*}M from Examples 3.2.

Now let LL be the image of the zero section ZZ under the time 11 flow of vFv_{F}. It is easy to see that ZZ and LL are exact Lagrangian submanifolds of the exact symplectic manifold T∗​MT^{*}M. Thus, Floer homology is well defined. Floer [24] showed that rigid flow lines of the gradient of ff are in correspondence with pseudo-holomorphic strips in the Floer complex of LL and ZZ. From this, one can show that the Floer homology of ZZ and LL is isomorphic to the Morse homology of MM:

F​H​(Z,L)≅H​(M;ℤ).FH(Z,L)\cong H(M;\mathbb{Z}).

This leads to the following fundamental result.

Theorem 3.26.

If ϕ\phi is a Hamiltonian diffeomorphism of T∗​MT^{*}M then

|(ϕ​(Z)∩Z)|≥∑i=0ndim(Hi​(M;ℤ))|(\phi(Z)\cap Z)|\geq\sum_{i=0}^{n}\dim(H_{i}(M;\mathbb{Z}))

where nn is the dimension of MM.

Proof.

We know that the dimension of a chain complex is greater than or equal to the dimension of its homology, and the dimension of F​C​(Z,L)FC(Z,L) is |(ϕ​(Z)∩Z)||(\phi(Z)\cap Z)|, so

|(ϕ​(Z)∩Z)|≥dimF​H​(Z,L)≅dimH​(M;ℤ).|(\phi(Z)\cap Z)|\geq\dim FH(Z,L)\cong\dim H(M;\mathbb{Z}).

This establishes the theorem. ∎

We now have the following famous corollary of Gromov [28] (though Gromov’s proof was very different).

Theorem 3.27.

There is no closed exact Lagrangian submanifold in (ℝ2​n,ωs​t​d)(\mathbb{R}^{2n},\omega_{std}). (See Examples 3.1 for the symplectic structure on ℝ2​n\mathbb{R}^{2n}.)

Proof.

Suppose there was a closed exact Lagrangian submanifold LL in (ℝ2​n,ωs​t​d)(\mathbb{R}^{2n},\omega_{std}). Let f:ℝ2​n→ℝf\colon\thinspace\mathbb{R}^{2n}\to\mathbb{R} be the projection onto the x1x_{1}-axis. Then the flow of the Hamiltonian vector field vfv_{f} is translation in the y1y_{1}-direction. Thus there is a Hamiltonian diffeomorphism ϕ:ℝ2​n→ℝ2​n\phi\colon\thinspace\mathbb{R}^{2n}\to\mathbb{R}^{2n} such that ϕ​(L)∩L=∅\phi(L)\cap L=\emptyset. This implies that F​H​(L,ϕ​(L))=0FH(L,\phi(L))=0 but from the previous theorem, and the fact that any Lagrangian submanifold has a neighborhood symplectomorphic to a neighborhood of the zero section in its cotangent bundle, we know that F​H​(L,ϕ​(L))FH(L,\phi(L)) must be isomorphic to H​(L;ℤ)H(L;\mathbb{Z}) which is not trivial. Thus, no such LL exists. ∎

3.5.2. Heegaard Floer homology

We now discuss an application of Lagrangian Floer homology to 33-manifold topology. We only give a hint at this application and refer the reader to [39] for all the details.

Given a closed oriented 33-manifold MM, there is always a surface Σ\Sigma of some genus gg that splits MM into two genus gg handlebodies. By this we mean that M∖Σ=V0∪V1M\setminus\Sigma=V_{0}\cup V_{1} and there are gg disjoint curves α1,…,αg\alpha_{1},\ldots,\alpha_{g} on Σ\Sigma such that each αi\alpha_{i} bounds a disk AiA_{i} in V0V_{0} such that V0∖∪i=1gAiV_{0}\setminus\cup_{i=1}^{g}A_{i} is a ball, and simlarly there are disjoint curves β1,…,βg\beta_{1},\ldots,\beta_{g} on Σ\Sigma such that each βi\beta_{i} bounds a disk BiB_{i} in V1V_{1} such that V1∖∪i=1gBiV_{1}\setminus\cup_{i=1}^{g}B_{i} is a ball. This is called a Heegaard splitting of MM. We note that the triple (Σ,{αi},{βi})(\Sigma,\{\alpha_{i}\},\{\beta_{i}\}) determines MM, so if we can associate an invariant to this tripple then it might determine an invariant of MM. It turns out that different triples can give the same MM, so to really get an invariant of MM we need to make sure that all triples for MM give the same result.

Notice that choosing an area form on Σ\Sigma, we see that Σ\Sigma is a symplectic manifold and that any curve on Σ\Sigma is Lagrangian. Thus the gg-fold product of Σ\Sigma with itself is a symplectic manifold and the products α1×⋯×αg\alpha_{1}\times\cdots\times\alpha_{g} and β1×⋯×βg\beta_{1}\times\cdots\times\beta_{g} are Lagrangian tori in this symplectic manifold. So we could try to apply Lagrangian Floer theory to these two tori, but this is not what we will do. We notice is that different labelings of the αi\alpha_{i} and βi\beta_{i} determine the same manifold MM, and we would like our construction not to depend on the ordering of these curves. To this end, we consider a quotient of the gg-fold product of Σ\Sigma by the symmetric group SgS_{g} (acting by permutting the coordinates). This is called the symmetric product and denoted Symg⁡(Σ)\operatorname{{Sym}}^{g}(\Sigma). We can now look at the image of the above two tori in Symg⁡(Σ)\operatorname{{Sym}}^{g}(\Sigma) and denote them by 𝕋α\mathbb{T}_{\alpha} and 𝕋β\mathbb{T}_{\beta}. With a little work, one can show that Symg⁡(Σ)\operatorname{{Sym}}^{g}(\Sigma) is also a symplectic manifold and the tori 𝕋α\mathbb{T}_{\alpha} and 𝕋β\mathbb{T}_{\beta} are Lagrangian. Now our symplectic manifold and Lagrangian submanifolds do not depend on the ordering of the αi\alpha_{i} and βi\beta_{i}. With a great deal of effort one can show that the Lagrangian Floer homology of 𝕋α\mathbb{T}_{\alpha} and 𝕋β\mathbb{T}_{\beta} is well-defined, so now we could define an invariant of the triple to be the Lagrangian Floer homology

F​H​(𝕋α,𝕋β).FH(\mathbb{T}_{\alpha},\mathbb{T}_{\beta}).

This turns out to be an invariant of MM, but not a particularly interesting one. To get an interesting invariant of MM, we need to add one more thing. If one fixes a point z∈Σz\in\Sigma that is disjoint from the αi\alpha_{i} and βi\beta_{i} then we can consider the image of {z}×Symg−1⁡(Σ)\{z\}\times\operatorname{{Sym}}^{g-1}(\Sigma) in Symg⁡(Σ)\operatorname{{Sym}}^{g}(\Sigma), denoted by FzF_{z}. This is a symplectic submanifold, and we can modify the differential in the Floer complex to take account of this submanifold in various ways. For example, we could ignore pseudo-holomorphic strips that intersect FzF_{z}, or we could add a formal variable to the coefficient ring of the chain complexes and then include a power of this variable that counts the intersections of the pseudo-holomorphic strips with FzF_{z}. One may check that doing either of these things will still produce a chain complex, and the homology of these complexes produces a powerful invariant of MM. (Of course, to see it is an invariant of MM, one needs to check that different triples and different choices of zz lead to the same homologies.) One may find details of all of these facts above in [39] and some of the first applications in [38]. A nice, and more details, survey of the construction can be found in [40].

4. Legendrian contact homology

We end this survey by discussing Legendrian contact homology. This is a version of Floer homology for Legendrian submanifolds of contact manifolds. It is a powerful invariant of Legendrian submanifolds and was first developed by Chekanov [6] and Eliashberg [14] in dimension 33, with the latter also discussing the higher-dimensional situation. They were able to show that there were Legendrian knots in ℝ3\mathbb{R}^{3} that had the same classical invariants but were not Legendrian isotopic because they had different Legendrian contact homology. For more details on applications of Legendrian contact homology, please see [18]. The theory was developed for certain contact manifolds in higher dimensions in [8, 11] and can be used to produce powerful (complete!) invariants of smooth knots in ℝ3\mathbb{R}^{3}, see [12].

We begin this section by discussing a few facts about contact geometry and Legendrian submanifolds. In the next section, we define Legendrian contact homology. A key feature, and the reason why we are discussing this here, is that the compactness issues that show up in this context do not allow us to define a homology theory as a vector space, but it will need to be an algebra. So we will see that we can follow the same program to create a homology theory as we did above, but the compactness issues will dictate the algebraic structure necessary to define our chain groups. We then end with a section discussing applications of Legendrian contact homology.

4.1. A little contact geometry

There are many good introductions to contact geometry, for example [15, 19, 26]. We refer the reader to those sources for a more comprehensive introduction to the subject, but discuss a few basic definitions and results here to set the stage for Lagrangian contact homology.

A contact manifold is a pair (M,ξ)(M,\xi), where MM is a manifold of dimension 2​n+12n+1, and ξ\xi is a sub-bundle of the tangent bundle T​MTM, that has fibers of dimension 2​n2n and is totally non-integrable. That is, ξ\xi is a hyperplane field on MM, and being totally non-integrable means that if it is tangent to a submanifold of MM along an open set of the submanifold, then the submanifold has dimension less than or equal to nn. One may use the Frobenius theorem to show that the total non-integrability is equivalent to ξ\xi being (locally) defined by a 11-form α\alpha (that is ξ=ker⁡α\xi=\ker\alpha) and

α∧(d​α)n\alpha\wedge(d\alpha)^{n}

is a never zero 2​n+12n+1-form.

Example 4.1.

Let (X,d​λ)(X,d\lambda) be an exact symplectic manifold. Then the manifold M=X×ℝM=X\times\mathbb{R} has a contact structure ξλ\xi_{\lambda} given by the kernel of d​t−λdt-\lambda, where tt is the coordinate on ℝ\mathbb{R}.

A specific example of this construction is M=T∗​W×ℝM=T^{*}W\times\mathbb{R} where we use the Liouville form λs​t​d\lambda_{std} on the cotangent bundle T∗​WT^{*}W.

An even more specific example is ℝ2​n+1=T∗​ℝn×ℝ\mathbb{R}^{2n+1}=T^{*}\mathbb{R}^{n}\times\mathbb{R} with the contact form α=d​t−∑i=1nyi​d​xi\alpha=dt-\sum_{i=1}^{n}y_{i}\,dx_{i}, where the xix_{i} are coodinates on ℝn\mathbb{R}^{n} and the yiy_{i} are coordinates on the fibers of T∗​ℝnT^{*}\mathbb{R}^{n}. See Figure 21 for the case when n=1n=1.

A submanifold LL of a (2​n+1)(2n+1)-dimensional contact manifold (M,ξ)(M,\xi) is called Legendrian if it has dimension nn and the tangent space of LL is contained in ξ\xi: Tx​L⊂ξxT_{x}L\subset\xi_{x} for all x∈Lx\in L. Legendrian submanifolds are essential objects in contact geometry, see for example, [16, 17].

Suppose that LL is a Legendrian submanifold of (X×ℝ,d​t−λ)(X\times\mathbb{R},dt-\lambda) and let π:(X×ℝ)→X\pi\colon\thinspace(X\times\mathbb{R})\to X be the projection map.

Exercise 4.2.

Show that π​(L)\pi(L) is an immersed exact Lagrangian submanifold of the symplectic manifold (X,−d​λ)(X,-d\lambda).

The idea of Legendrian contact homology is to try to “do” Lagrangian Floer homology with π​(L)\pi(L). We will see below that this does not work, but with some modification, we can build a different homology theory that does work.

4.2. Defining Legendrian contact homology

As mentioned at the end of the last section, we would like to try to define some form of Lagrangian Floer homology for π​(L)\pi(L) where LL is a compact Legendrian submanifold of (X×ℝ,d​t−λ)(X\times\mathbb{R},dt-\lambda) and π:(X×ℝ)→X\pi\colon\thinspace(X\times\mathbb{R})\to X is the projection map. To that end, observe that, possibly after a small perturbation, we can assume that π​(L)\pi(L) is embedded except for a finite number of transverse double points. We could then try to define a complex as a vector space generated by the double points of π​(L)\pi(L) and then, after choosing a complex structure JJ compatible with −d​λ-d\lambda, define a differential by counting pseudo-holomorphic strips between double points. We begin by seeing that this does not work!

We will consider the situation where X=T∗​ℝX=T^{*}\mathbb{R} (though the same considerations occur in the more general situation). Consider a pseudo-holomorphic map u:(ℝ×[0,1])→ℝ2u\colon\thinspace(\mathbb{R}\times[0,1])\to\mathbb{R}^{2} such that the boundary of ℝ×[0,1]\mathbb{R}\times[0,1] maps to π​(L)\pi(L) and as one approaches infinity, the map limits to double points. Notice that this map is a pseudo-holomorphic map from ℝ×[0,1]\mathbb{R}\times[0,1] to ℝ2\mathbb{R}^{2}, so we can use our knowledge of single variable complex analysis to study this map. We first note that if there is a non-regular point of uu on the interior of ℝ×[0,1]\mathbb{R}\times[0,1], then generically, we can assume it is of the form z↦znz\mapsto z^{n} and, if n>2n>2, by a small perturbation, the critical point will break into several critical points which have local model z↦z2z\mapsto z^{2}. So, assuming our critical point has a local model of the form z↦z2z\mapsto z^{2}, we note that there is a 22-parameter family of maps near uu with the same image that come by moving the critical point (since ℝ2\mathbb{R}^{2} is 22-dimensional). We also note that we still have the ℝ\mathbb{R} actions on such maps, so we really have a 22-dimensional family, even after modding out by this ℝ\mathbb{R} action. Since we are only interested in strips in 11-dimensional families (see the section on Lagrangian Floer homology), we can assume the maps uu we are interested in do not have interior critical points.

Now consider a critical point on the boundary. Generically, we can assume it is also of the form z↦z2z\mapsto z^{2}, but now the critical points must map to π​(L)\pi(L), and thus, there will be a 11-parameter family of maps near uu. So again, if we are interested in rigid maps, we will only consider maps uu with no critical points on the boundary or interior. That is, uu is an immersion. However, when trying to prove ∂2=0\partial^{2}=0, we will need to consider 11-parameter familes and so will consider uu with a critical point on its boundary.

Now suppose xx, yy, and zz are double points of π​(L)\pi(L) and u:(ℝ×[0,1])→ℝ2u\colon\thinspace(\mathbb{R}\times[0,1])\to\mathbb{R}^{2} is a rigid pseudo-holomorphic strip from xx to yy with boundary in π​(L)\pi(L) and u′:(ℝ×[0,1])→ℝ2u^{\prime}\colon\thinspace(\mathbb{R}\times[0,1])\to\mathbb{R}^{2} is a similar strip from yy to zz. So if we defined our homology theory like in the case of Floer homology, then zz would be counted in ∂2x\partial^{2}x. We can see such a situation in Figure 22.

Figure 23 shows the family of disks formed when uu and u′u^{\prime} are glued.

We notice that this family will now break into pseudo-holomorphic disks as shown in Figure 24.

So we see that the boundary of the compactified moduli spaces has a broken pseudo-holomorphic strip made out of two rigid pseudo-holomorphic strips and also a broken disk made out of a pseudo-holomorphic disk going through three double points and another pseudo-holomorphic disk going through only one double point.

Let us formalize these sorts of pseudo-holomorphic disks. Let p0,…,pkp_{0},\ldots,p_{k} be k+1k+1 points arranged anti-clockwise round the boundary of the unit disk D2D^{2} in ℝ2\mathbb{R}^{2}, and let Dk+1D_{k+1} be D2−{p0,…,pk}D^{2}-\{p_{0},\ldots,p_{k}\}. We will consider maps

u:Dk+1→ℝ2u\colon\thinspace D_{k+1}\to\mathbb{R}^{2}

with u​(∂Dk+1)⊂π​(L)u(\partial D_{k+1})\subset\pi(L). We will be interested in such maps where the limits as we approach one of the pip_{i} is a double point of π​(L)\pi(L). Moreover, we call a puncture pip_{i} of uu positive if as we transverse ∂Dk+1\partial D_{k+1} in the anti-clockwise direction we move from a lower sheet of π​(L)\pi(L) to an upper sheet of π​(L)\pi(L) and we will call the puncture negative if we move from an upper sheet to a lower sheet.

In this language, we see that if we “glue” two rigid pseudo-holomorphic disks from D2D_{2} to ℝ2\mathbb{R}^{2}, we can obtain a 11-parameter family of disks whose other boundary component consists of pseudo-holomorphic disks with domain D3D_{3} and D1D_{1}. So it is clear that the formalism in the definition of Floer homology for a pair of Lagrangian submanifolds from the last section will not work here! Moreover, it is not hard to see that if we also consider disks with 33 punctures, then in 11-parameter families we will also see disks with more punctures on their boundaries. We do note that if we glue a rigid pseudo-holomorphic disk with nn punctures, one being positive and the other negative, to a disk with mm punctures of the same type, then we get a 11-parameter family of pseudo-holomorphic disks with punctures that will have boundary two disks with punctures.

So if we try to define a differential of a double point in π​(L)\pi(L) we need to consider all such disks. So the differential of a double point xx will need to count disks with a positive puncture at xx and negative punctures at x1,…,xkx_{1},\ldots,x_{k}. Then the chain complex cannot be a vector space generated by the double points, but it will need to be an algebra generated by the double points so that the boundary consists of words in the double points. So we see that the compactness issues in the problem force us to choose an algebraic structure for the chain complex when trying to define a homology theory.

Based on the above observations, we can now give the definition of Legendrian contact homology for Legendrian knots in X×ℝX\times\mathbb{R} where XX is an exact symplectic manifold. To this end, if LL is such a Legendrian submanifold, we let the chain complex C​(L)C(L) be the free algebra over RR generated by the double points of π​(L)\pi(L), where RR is the ring ℤ/2​ℤ\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z} or ZZ (there are more sophisticated rings as well).

We will now define a grading on C​(L)C(L). To simplify this, we assume that the tangent space of XX can be globally trivialized: T​X≅X×ℂnTX\cong X\times\mathbb{C}^{n}. This assumption is not necessary, but without it, the definition is more complicated. Now, given a double point x∈π​(L)x\in\pi(L); there are two points in LL that map to xx. Call the one with the larger ℝ\mathbb{R}-coordinate x+x^{+} and the other x−x^{-}. Choose a path γx:[0,1]→L\gamma_{x}\colon\thinspace[0,1]\to L from x+x^{+} to x−x^{-}. Notice that Tπ​(γx​(t))​π​(L)T_{\pi(\gamma_{x}(t))}\pi(L) is a Lagrangian subspace of Tπ​(γx​(t))​X=ℂnT_{\pi(\gamma_{x}(t))}X=\mathbb{C}^{n}. Because the tangent bundle of XX is trivial, we obtain a path of Lagrangians in ℂn\mathbb{C}^{n}. We can make this path a loop as we did in Section 3.4.1 above. The Conley-Zehnder index ν​(x)\nu(x) of xx is simply the Maslov index of this loop of Lagrangian subspaces. Notice that this number depends on the path γ\gamma we chose. To deal with this, we notice that we can get a loop of Lagrangian subspaces in ℂn\mathbb{C}^{n} from any loop ll in LL. By taking the Maslov index of the loop of Lagrangian subspaces associated to π​(l)\pi(l), we obtain a map μ:H1​(L)→ℤ\mu\colon\thinspace H_{1}(L)\to\mathbb{Z}. Let c​(L)c(L) be the smallest positive integer in the image of this map. It is not hard to see that ν​(x)\nu(x) is well-defined modulo c​(L)c(L). Finally, we define the grading of a double point xx to be |x|=ν​(x)−1|x|=\nu(x)-1, and the grading of a word in double points is the sum of the gradings of the elements in the word. Our grading is well-defined modulo c​(L)c(L).

To define the differential of a point pp, we consider pseudo-holomorphic maps u:Dk+1→Xu\colon\thinspace D_{k+1}\to X with u​(∂Dk+1)⊂π​(L)u(\partial D_{k+1})\subset\pi(L), with the puncture p0p_{0} being positive and mapping to x0x_{0} and the other punctures being negative where pip_{i} maps to xix_{i}. Denote the moduli space of such maps, modulo pseudo-holomorphic reparameterization, by ℳx1,…,xkx0\mathcal{M}^{x_{0}}_{x_{1},\ldots,x_{k}}.

(We note that the dimension in this theorem is only well-defined modulo c​(L)c(L), since the grading is only defined modulo c​(L)c(L). This just means that the moduli space can have components of dimension |x0|−∑i=1k|xi|−1+n​c​(L)|x_{0}|-\sum_{i=1}^{k}|x_{i}|-1+nc(L) for any integer nn.)

For our compactness result, we need to see to what a sequence of pseudo-holomorphic disks in ℳx1,…,xkx0\mathcal{M}^{x_{0}}_{x_{1},\ldots,x_{k}} can converge. To this end, we define a broken pseudo-holomorphic disk. Given an element u∈ℳx1,…,xkx0u\in\mathcal{M}^{x_{0}}_{x_{1},\ldots,x_{k}} and v∈ℳxk+1,…,xlxiv\in\mathcal{M}^{x_{i}}_{x_{k+1},\ldots,x_{l}} for some ii between 11 and kk and some l≥kl\geq k, we call the union of uu and vv a broken pseudo-holomorphic disks. We can define a sequence of disks unu_{n} in ℳx1,…,xi−1,xk+1,…,xl,xi+1,…,xkx0\mathcal{M}^{x_{0}}_{x_{1},\ldots,x_{i-1},x_{k+1},\ldots,x_{l},x_{i+1},\ldots,x_{k}} converging to the broken disk defined by uu and vv just as we did in Section 3.3. See Figure 25.

We now have our compactness result.

Remark 4.3.

We note that, for simplicity, this theorem is not quite accurate in some cases, as we defined ℳx1,…,xkx0\mathcal{M}^{x_{0}}_{x_{1},\ldots,x_{k}} to be pseudo-holomorphic disks moded out by pseudo-holomorphic reparameterization. When kk is large, there are no such reparameterizations and the theorem is correct as stated, but for small kk, the statement should be made before one considers reparameterization.

Just as we did while discussing Floer homology, we roughly state the gluing theorem as follows.

We are now ready to define our differential. We will define it on generators and then extend to the algebra using linearity and a signed Leibniz rule

∂(w1​w2)=(∂w1)​w2+(−1)|w1|​w1​∂(w2).\partial(w_{1}w_{2})=(\partial w_{1})w_{2}+(-1)^{|w_{1}|}w_{1}\partial(w_{2}).

Let xx be a double point of π​(L)\pi(L), that is, a generator of C​(L)C(L). We define the differential of xx to be

∂x=∑x1,…,xk|x|−∑i=1k|xi|=1|ℳx1,…,xkx|​x1​⋯​xk,\partial x=\sum_{\begin{subarray}{c}x_{1},\ldots,x_{k}\\ |x|-\sum_{i=1}^{k}|x_{i}|=1\end{subarray}}|\mathcal{M}^{x}_{x_{1},\ldots,x_{k}}|\,x_{1}\cdots x_{k},

where |ℳx1,…,xkx||\mathcal{M}^{x}_{x_{1},\ldots,x_{k}}| is the signed count of the 0 dimensional components of ℳx1,…,xkx\mathcal{M}^{x}_{x_{1},\ldots,x_{k}}, and kk is any non-negative integer.

Arguing as in previous sections, we can easily check the following result.

Theorem 4.4.

The differential ∂:C​(L)→C​(L)\partial\colon\thinspace C(L)\to C(L) lowers the grading by 11 and satisfies ∂2=0\partial^{2}=0.

We can now define the Legendrian contact homology to be

L​C​H​(L)=ker⁡(∂:C​(L)→C​(L))image⁡(∂:C​(L)→C​(L)).LCH(L)=\frac{\ker(\partial\colon\thinspace C(L)\to C(L))}{\operatorname{image}(\partial\colon\thinspace C(L)\to C(L))}.

Moreover, we can also prove the invariance of the homology under Legendrian isotopy and the choice of complex structure JJ, by using alternate versions of Legendrian Floer Theorems 1 through 4 in a similar way we proved that Morse homology is independent of the chosen metric and Morse function.

Theorem 4.5.

If LL and L′L^{\prime} are Legendrian isotopic, then L​C​H​(L)≅L​C​H​(L′)LCH(L)\cong LCH(L^{\prime}).

4.3. Applications

It was an open question whether two Legendrian knots in the standard contact structure on ℝ3\mathbb{R}^{3} that were smoothly isotopic and had the same “classical invariants” (these are invariants associated with the tangent information of a Legendrian knot) had to be Legendrian isotopic. In the late 1990s, Chekanov [6] and Eliashberg [14] defined Legendrian contact homology and used it to show that such Legendrian knots do indeed exist. It turns out that it is very difficult to work with L​C​H​(L)LCH(L) since it is a non-abelian object. So it was important to be able to extract computable information from this difficult algebraic object. This was done using “augmentations” and ”linearizations”. We refer the reader to [18] for a recent overview of this whole theory, along with several computations. In a series of papers [8, 9, 10, 11], Ekholm, Sullivan, and the author developed this theory in higher dimensions and also used it to distinguish many Legendrian submanifolds.

There is another interesting and surprising application of Legendrian contact homology. Specifically, one can use it to study and distinguish smooth knots in ℝ3\mathbb{R}^{3}! This actually works for submanifolds in any dimensions, but we will restrict our discussion to knots in ℝ3\mathbb{R}^{3}. We first construct our contact manifold. If we fix a metric on ℝ3\mathbb{R}^{3} and look at the unit cotangent bundle U∗​ℝ3⊂T∗​ℝ3U^{*}\mathbb{R}^{3}\subset T^{*}\mathbb{R}^{3}, we can restrict the Liouville form λs​t​d\lambda_{std} on T∗​ℝ3T^{*}\mathbb{R}^{3} to obtain a contact form on U∗​ℝ3U^{*}\mathbb{R}^{3}. Now, if KK is a knot in ℝ3\mathbb{R}^{3}, then we can consider U∗​(K)U^{*}(K), the unit conormal bundles

U∗​(K)={α∈Ux∗​ℝ3:x∈K​ and ​α​ vanishes on Tx​K}.U^{*}(K)=\{\alpha\in U_{x}^{*}\mathbb{R}^{3}:x\in K\text{ and }\alpha\text{ vanishes on $T_{x}K$}\}.

One may easily check that U∗​(K)U^{*}(K) is a Legendrian torus in U∗​ℝ3U^{*}\mathbb{R}^{3}; and, moreover, if KK and K′K^{\prime} are smoothly isotopic then U∗​(K)U^{*}(K) and U∗​(K′)U^{*}(K^{\prime}) are Legendrian isotopic. Thus, the Legendrian contact homology of U∗​(K)U^{*}(K) is an invariant of the smooth isotopy class of KK! It is known that this is almost a complete invariant (and might be a complete invariant) [13]. For more on this story, see [12].

References

  • [1] Alberto Abbondandolo and Felix Schlenk. Floer homologies, with applications. Jahresber. Dtsch. Math.-Ver., 121(3):155–238, 2019.
  • [2] Michèle Audin and Mihai Damian. Morse theory and Floer homology. Universitext. Springer, London; EDP Sciences, Les Ulis, 2014. Translated from the 2010 French original by Reinie Erné.
  • [3] Augustin Banyaga and David Hurtubise. Lectures on Morse homology, volume 29 of Kluwer Texts in the Mathematical Sciences. Kluwer Academic Publishers Group, Dordrecht, 2004.
  • [4] Raoul Bott. Morse theory indomitable. Inst. Hautes Études Sci. Publ. Math., (68):99–114, 1988.
  • [5] Ana Cannas da Silva. Lectures on symplectic geometry, volume 1764 of Lecture Notes in Mathematics. Springer-Verlag, Berlin, 2001.
  • [6] Yuri Chekanov. Differential algebra of Legendrian links. Invent. Math., 150(3):441–483, 2002.
  • [7] S. K. Donaldson. Floer homology groups in Yang-Mills theory, volume 147 of Cambridge Tracts in Mathematics. Cambridge University Press, Cambridge, 2002. With the assistance of M. Furuta and D. Kotschick.
  • [8] Tobias Ekholm, John Etnyre, and Michael Sullivan. The contact homology of Legendrian submanifolds in ℝ2​n+1{\mathbb{R}}^{2n+1}. J. Differential Geom., 71(2):177–305, 2005.
  • [9] Tobias Ekholm, John Etnyre, and Michael Sullivan. Non-isotopic Legendrian submanifolds in ℝ2​n+1\mathbb{R}^{2n+1}. J. Differential Geom., 71(1):85–128, 2005.
  • [10] Tobias Ekholm, John Etnyre, and Michael Sullivan. Orientations in Legendrian contact homology and exact Lagrangian immersions. Internat. J. Math., 16(5):453–532, 2005.
  • [11] Tobias Ekholm, John Etnyre, and Michael Sullivan. Legendrian contact homology in P×ℝP\times\mathbb{R}. Trans. Amer. Math. Soc., 359(7):3301–3335, 2007.
  • [12] Tobias Ekholm, John B. Etnyre, Lenhard Ng, and Michael G. Sullivan. Knot contact homology. Geom. Topol., 17(2):975–1112, 2013.
  • [13] Tobias Ekholm, Lenhard Ng, and Vivek Shende. A complete knot invariant from contact homology. Invent. Math., 211(3):1149–1200, 2018.
  • [14] Yakov Eliashberg. Invariants in contact topology. In Proceedings of the International Congress of Mathematicians, Vol. II (Berlin, 1998), number Extra Vol. II, pages 327–338 (electronic), 1998.
  • [15] John B. Etnyre. Introductory lectures on contact geometry. In Topology and geometry of manifolds (Athens, GA, 2001), volume 71 of Proc. Sympos. Pure Math., pages 81–107. Amer. Math. Soc., Providence, RI, 2003.
  • [16] John B. Etnyre. Legendrian and transversal knots. In Handbook of knot theory, pages 105–185. Elsevier B. V., Amsterdam, 2005.
  • [17] John B. Etnyre. Submanifolds in contact geometry. arXiv 2510.05804, 2025.
  • [18] John B. Etnyre and Lenhard L. Ng. Legendrian contact homology in ℝ3\mathbb{R}^{3}. In Surveys in differential geometry 2020. Surveys in 3-manifold topology and geometry, volume 25 of Surv. Differ. Geom., pages 103–161. Int. Press, Boston, MA, [2022] ©2022.
  • [19] John B. Etnyre and Bülent Tosun. Low dimensional contact geometry. To appear.
  • [20] A. Floer. Monopoles on asymptotically flat manifolds. In The Floer memorial volume, volume 133 of Progr. Math., pages 3–41. Birkhäuser, Basel, 1995.
  • [21] Andreas Floer. Morse theory for fixed points of symplectic diffeomorphisms. Bull. Amer. Math. Soc. (N.S.), 16(2):279–281, 1987.
  • [22] Andreas Floer. An instanton-invariant for 33-manifolds. Comm. Math. Phys., 118(2):215–240, 1988.
  • [23] Andreas Floer. Morse theory for Lagrangian intersections. J. Differential Geom., 28(3):513–547, 1988.
  • [24] Andreas Floer. Witten’s complex and infinite-dimensional Morse theory. J. Differential Geom., 30(1):207–221, 1989.
  • [25] Urs Frauenfelder and Robert Nicholls. The moduli space of gradient flow lines and morse homology, 2020.
  • [26] Hansjörg Geiges. An introduction to contact topology, volume 109 of Cambridge Studies in Advanced Mathematics. Cambridge University Press, Cambridge, 2008.
  • [27] M. Golubitsky and V. Guillemin. Stable mappings and their singularities, volume Vol. 14 of Graduate Texts in Mathematics. Springer-Verlag, New York-Heidelberg, 1973.
  • [28] M. Gromov. Pseudoholomorphic curves in symplectic manifolds. Invent. Math., 82(2):307–347, 1985.
  • [29] Victor Guillemin and Alan Pollack. Differential topology. AMS Chelsea Publishing, Providence, RI, 2010. Reprint of the 1974 original.
  • [30] Michael Hutchings. An index inequality for embedded pseudoholomorphic curves in symplectizations. J. Eur. Math. Soc. (JEMS), 4(4):313–361, 2002.
  • [31] Michael Hutchings. Lecture notes on morse homology (with an eye towards floer theory and pseudoholomorphic curves). Lecture notes, https://math.berkeley.edu/ hutching/teach/276-2010/mfp.ps, 2002.
  • [32] Michael Hutchings. Lecture notes on embedded contact homology. In Contact and symplectic topology, volume 26 of Bolyai Soc. Math. Stud., pages 389–484. János Bolyai Math. Soc., Budapest, 2014.
  • [33] Peter Kronheimer and Tomasz Mrowka. Monopoles and three-manifolds, volume 10 of New Mathematical Monographs. Cambridge University Press, Cambridge, 2007.
  • [34] Peter Kronheimer and Tomasz Mrowka. Knots, sutures, and excision. J. Differential Geom., 84(2):301–364, 2010.
  • [35] John M. Lee. Introduction to smooth manifolds, volume 218 of Graduate Texts in Mathematics. Springer, New York, second edition, 2013.
  • [36] Dusa McDuff and Dietmar Salamon. Introduction to symplectic topology. Oxford Mathematical Monographs. The Clarendon Press Oxford University Press, New York, second edition, 1998.
  • [37] J. Milnor. Morse theory, volume No. 51 of Annals of Mathematics Studies. Princeton University Press, Princeton, NJ, 1963. Based on lecture notes by M. Spivak and R. Wells.
  • [38] Peter Ozsváth and Zoltán Szabó. Holomorphic disks and three-manifold invariants: properties and applications. Ann. of Math. (2), 159(3):1159–1245, 2004.
  • [39] Peter Ozsváth and Zoltán Szabó. Holomorphic disks and topological invariants for closed three-manifolds. Ann. of Math. (2), 159(3):1027–1158, 2004.
  • [40] Peter Ozsváth and Zoltán Szabó. Lectures on Heegaard Floer homology. In Floer homology, gauge theory, and low-dimensional topology, volume 5 of Clay Math. Proc., pages 29–70. Amer. Math. Soc., Providence, RI, 2006.
  • [41] Joel Robbin and Dietmar Salamon. The Maslov index for paths. Topology, 32(4):827–844, 1993.
  • [42] Matthias Schwarz. Morse homology, volume 111 of Progress in Mathematics. Birkhäuser Verlag, Basel, 1993.

Instructions for reporting errors

We are continuing to improve HTML versions of papers, and your feedback helps enhance accessibility and mobile support. To report errors in the HTML that will help us improve conversion and rendering, choose any of the methods listed below:

  • Click the "Report Issue" () button, located in the page header.

Tip: You can select the relevant text first, to include it in your report.

Our team has already identified the following issues. We appreciate your time reviewing and reporting rendering errors we may not have found yet. Your efforts will help us improve the HTML versions for all readers, because disability should not be a barrier to accessing research. Thank you for your continued support in championing open access for all.

Have a free development cycle? Help support accessibility at arXiv! Our collaborators at LaTeXML maintain a list of packages that need conversion, and welcome developer contributions.
